The choice of uuid1 is actually on purpose as due to the involvement of the host unique address, timestamp and sequence it ensures a uniqueness across distributed systems. UUID4 has a very low but possible chance for collisions as it's totally randomic.

Also uuid4 relies on hardware based entropy, which means that in some very rare conditions it might block until new entropy is available.

I think that allowing an option to provide a custom nodeid would avoid the leakage of the MAC address, even though it would still allow to guess the sequence of IDs, which I don't feel it's an issue given that depot is supposed to serve public files.

> I also don't get why I'd want to use JSX when es6 template literals are so powerful.

JSX is zero-cost — in the C++/Rust sense, it's compiled to creating the corresponding objects directly — and I think more clear/flexible, you can have JSX inside JS inside JSX since JSX is ultimately just compiled to JS (so you can e.g. map an array to a JSX node). ES6 templates are powerful but they have to be entirely processed at runtime and nesting templates inside templates isn't for the faint of heart.

Also note that you can use React (and friends I assume) without JSX by calling the relevant functions/constructors directly.

Uhm, I understood this issue to mostly be:

> it would be easier for the packagers to keep the mandatory dependencies to the minimum

(btw, thanks again for your work on keeping the arch package up to date!)

Rather than removing features, I'd like to help with the packaging effort, so I dusted off my Arch VM (gosh, I haven't used it since June 2011, and it was a pain to update :/ ) and I was ready to contribute a small PKGBUILD for resumable_urlretrieve

But then I realized that it has already been pushed to AUR... I then installed [aura](https://github.com/aurapm/aura) and used it to install `python-pew`

And everything works quite smoothly.

Also, while Arch is quite bleeding edge, and thus pythonz won't be used to install e.g. Python 3.5.1 ...apparently older versions aren't available in the main repositories (I'm sure they might be on AUR, though), and thus pythonz is still useful. (if pythonz had no use case on Arch, removing it might have been more acceptable)

So, I'm not quite sure about the problem you mentioned:

> for the archlinux package, since the package is not in the official repositories, when you try to generate the package, you get an error because the package depends on pythonz-bd. So you need to install pythonz-bd before, but then you get a new error, because pythonz-db depends on resumable-urlretrieve.

I mean: python-pew is not in the official repositories either, and thus the same tool that installs pew should be able to fetch pythonz and resumable_urlretrieve (and that's the case in my experience)

If you plan for them to be moved as extra dependencies, and then specify them as extra dependencies for Arch, this wouldn't really be any easier for you, am I right? And since afaik (maybe it changed in the last version) pip doesn't prominently tell you about optional dependencies, and that the syntax for installing optional stuff is not well known (`pip install package[option]`)... I feel like making this optional would simply make features disappear for the less savvy users (also, what happend when you `pip install --upgrade` the package? does it also upgrades the optional dependencies? if not this might lead to broken packages... I'd need to look into this)

Obviously I could try to educate users, and add an appropriate message (like "run pip install pythonz-bd, but only if you installed pew via pip") when the users invokes `pew install`

But if the issue is only the maintenance workload for the Arch (and potentially other distros) packager, I promise you that `resumable_urlretrieve` won't receive a lot of updates: it only needs to do 1 thing, and it's arguably better tested than `pew`. And for the couple of pythonz features used by pew, we don't need to really keep it up to date. Most updates are for the newer python versions, but pythonz is able to install them even without (it just won't list them as available and won't be able to check their sha256).

So, for me it'd be perfectly fine if the Arch pythonz-bd and resumable-urlretrieve packages will be lagging behind a bit

Well, it's quickest and simplest to just take a look at `CHANGELOG.md` that most projects provide these days. Or `HISTORY.rst`.

Going into git commits is nowhere near the convenience of simply taking a look at single file (in Github), that lists whole history of project in _human-readable_ form. `git log` contains bloat, bad/non-descriptive commit messages, etc. Plus if there are changes or upgrade instructions that you want to highlight, then you need a separate changelog.

Maintaining a changelog requires some effort (though not very much) but it's valuable to lazy/busy followers of the project.

OK cool, looking at that last change, this is getting pretty close to shippable. I'm pretty sure there's a Gödelian issue/feature we can make by maintaining the invariant that `S[T] != S`, but instead `T` continues to reference its previous value. The motivation behind this is twofold:

* You already have `S`, so `S[T]` equaling `S` kind of doesn't add anything.
* You can continue iterating while referring to the scope, so: `[(S, T.method(S['some_context']))]` continues to work. This probably isn't a very common pattern, but for us, and some future subset of power users, this pattern will help avoid having to write extensions and reduce extraneous `Call` usage.

> in that way libraries can be disabled without changing the main fobos file.

> Yes, this is could be viable.

> fobos.inc
>
> [section-of-any-name]lapack = /opt/arch/lapack3.1.1

> fobos

> [default]compiler = gnulibs = $fobos.inc(lapack) $fobos.inc(fft)

So if you type ```howdoi QUERY``` into the console, what howdoi does first is gathers links by doing a Google search:

>['https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/cat', 'http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/0679449531', 'http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/0811858227', 'http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/B000RIA95G', 'http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/B003E6WSHK', 'https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38177026/what-does-cat-file-stand-for-in-git', 'http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/B007KAZ166', 'https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40108569/how-to-get-the-last-line-of-a-file-using-cat-command', 'https://stackoverflow.com/questions/921455/how-do-i-program-using-cat', 'https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39942297/r-doesnt-append-lines-to-file-using-cat-or-write']

It then filters this array and returns a new array that only links to actual questions.

>['https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38177026/what-does-cat-file-stand-for-in-git', 'https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40108569/how-to-get-the-last-line-of-a-file-using-cat-command', 'https://stackoverflow.com/questions/921455/how-do-i-program-using-cat', 'https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39942297/r-doesnt-append-lines-to-file-using-cat-or-write']

...and then visit those links to grab the answers.

We use the first array (the one with rads.stackoverflow.com) when printing out the link to the answer in our answer header. We use the second array (the one *without* rads.stackoverflow.com) to actually find and print out the answer. These two arrays are vastly different...and would explain why I might get an answer that claims to be from https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/cat but is actually showing text from https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38177026/what-does-cat-file-stand-for-in-git.

For what reason you separate crawler and parse? Why not just parse it after fetch through message queue? If the download content are really large, you may consider running one fetcher and one processor in same processor and use language built-in queue to reduce network cost.

The number of projects will not affect component besides scheduler.

If you are actually using phantomjs, run more instance, it's quite slow and unstable. The number of fetcher depends on the number of tasks per second, and average fetch time: #tasks * avg_time / 100 you could also * 2~5

the number of result_workers depends on the time cost on database store operation. For example, each result needs 100ms and 20 results per second, you need at least 2 result worker instance. Another example, you need 200ms per result to store into database with 20 results per second, you need at least 4.

If the queue to fetch not choked, the priority of tasks are isolated between projects. Higher priority tasks executed first and only limited by tokens(traffic control algo used by pyspider), which mean:

* if you have unlimited queue size and tokens, all the task will scheduled immediately regardless the capability of fetcher, you will see 200,000+ tasks in the queue waiting to fetch unordered if you don't have enough fetcher.  But if you didn't see the tasks choked in queue, it will be fetched and processed very soon, and don't need to worry about priority.   
* If the tokens are limited, the higher priority task will be scheduled first.
* If the queue size are full, the tasks will be scheduled by internal order of projects. Example the queue size are 100k and tokens are unlimited, it may scheduler all the tasks in project 3 and project 4 (10k + 20k) and the first 70k tasks from project 1 ordered by priority.

On a modern Python it's definitely not sufficient to only set `SO_RCVTIMEO` when calling Python's `recv()`:

- `sock_recv_guts()` uses `sock_call()`: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b98f1715a35d2cbd1e9e45e1e7ae51a39e00dc4a/Modules/socketmodule.c#L3146
- `sock_call()` uses `sock_call_ex()` which implements its own timeout logic using non-blocking calls: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b98f1715a35d2cbd1e9e45e1e7ae51a39e00dc4a/Modules/socketmodule.c#L751 ("The connection runs asynchronously.")

1. Use an existing Raft implementation, there's a few Python libraries available after doing a quick GitHub search. Since the library is independent of the transport, it should be possible to use SQS or the HTTP listener for a transport layer.
1. Use etcd from CoreOS as an established Raft server, and add options to YaS3fs for using etcd for locks. Since etcd supports TTLs on data, it would work for timing out locks. While we're on the topic - wouldn't it be better to change unsigned types to signed types where it's safe to do so.
As far as I understand, signed integral arithmetics run much faster and enable compilers to do a lot of additional optimisations (first of all unsigned types are twice as slow due to compilers have to add mod operation for wrap_over guarantee and then compilers can't do most optimisations: https://kristerw.blogspot.co.uk/2016/02/how-undefined-signed-overflow-enables.html ).  
There is a potential danger of overflow of course, but considering that most of the code uses long, it's nearly impossible to overflow that.
There is also a danger of users passing negative values to functions that expect positive only values, but that could be solved with assertions.

Yeah, the drectangle is weird in that way.  But it's because the rectangle, that has integer coordinates, works this way.  And this makes them behave in the same way.  For example, a rectangle(1,1,1,1) is the rectangle that contains exactly the pixel point(1,1).  If it were defined the opencv way the integer rectangle object would have an odd API.  Since the dlib::rectangle has been around forever we can't change this even if we wanted to anyway.  

The first version of the drectangle I made behaved as you suggest, which is how you would expect something with continuous coordinates to work.  But then when it's converted to or from dlib::rectangle objects it gets all weird and wrong, giving bad and surprising behavior in a number of cases.  So I changed it to work the way it does now to make dlib::rectangle and dlib::drectangle compatible.

Although, the general use case for dlib::rectangle and dlib::drectangle is to represent boxes in images, where the coordinates are always integer valued and never in the range 0..1.   For that it's convenient.  But yeah, as a general rectangle the drectangle is weird.

PyBankID will not implement this. It can be done according to description below, but it is deemed to be a non-necessary feature for this implementation. Since the *self signed root certificate [...] is delivered to you on request*, I will leave the necessary instructions below for those wanting to verify signatures themselves.

From [BankID Relying Party Guidelines Version: 3.0 (2018-02-16)](https://www.bankid.com/assets/bankid/rp/bankid-relying-party-guidelines-v3.0.pdf):

## 12 Verifying Signatures
The signatures (including the certificates) returned from the service are already verified by the service.

**Note: The Relying Party does not need to verify the signatures**

It is however possible for the Relying Party to verify them. To do that, the following data is needed:
* The `signature` returned from the service. A specification of the content is delivered to you on
request.
* The certificate of the user and intermediate CA:s. These are included in the signature.
* The `ocspResponse` returned from the service.
* The self signed root certificate. This is delivered to you on request.

The following principle is applicable:
1. Verify the `signature`.
2. Verify the certificates in the certificate chain up to the self signed root. Note that certificates may have
expired at the time of verification if it is later than the time of use.
3. Verify the status of the `ocspResponse` to be ok.
4. Verify the signature of the `ocspResponse`.
5. Verify the certificate of the `ocspResponse` signer and that it is issued by the same CA as the user
certificate in question.
6. Verify the nonce included in the `ocspResponse` to be correct by matching it with a hash computed of
the signature. See *completionData for Completed Orders*.

Aha. So the issue is with the encrypted file keyring. The reason that a non-blank password is required is because encryption has no value if there's no secret information. That is, if you had a blank password, any adversary could trivially decrypt the keyring.

If you don't want to have any security on your keyring, I suggest instead configuring you environment to use the plain text keyring, which won't prompt for a password at all.

I see. It seems that although the packages are being included, the entry_points aren't being loaded, which may be a limitation of pyinstaller. I suspect if you were to run this code, you would find nothing resolves, instead of getting the list of backends in keyrings.alt:

```
>>> import pkg_resources
>>> print(list(pkg_resources.iter_entry_points('keyring.backends')))
[EntryPoint.parse('Gnome = keyrings.alt.Gnome'), EntryPoint.parse('kwallet = keyrings.alt.kwallet'), EntryPoint.parse('multi = keyrings.alt.multi'), EntryPoint.parse('Windows (alt) = keyrings.alt.Windows'), EntryPoint.parse('Google = keyrings.alt.Google'), EntryPoint.parse('pyfs = keyrings.alt.pyfs'), EntryPoint.parse('file = keyrings.alt.file'), EntryPoint.parse('keyczar = keyrings.alt.keyczar')]
```

Without this information, there's no way for keyrings.alt to inject its dependencies. This problem seems related slightly to pyinstaller/pyinstaller#1188.

It's possible that the package metadata in keyrings.alt that advertises the entry points either isn't included or pkg_resources is unable to resolve those entry points from the metadata when in the installer mode. You'll need to file this issue with pyinstaller. If there's something keyring or keyrings.alt can do to help, don't hesitate to respond back here.

I think it would fit in nicely in the [0.5 'New features' release](https://github.com/lawsie/guizero/projects/3). Just in case, here's a quick fix:

```python
import guizero as gz
import guizero.mixins as mix
import guizero.tkmixins as tkmix


class Window(
    mix.ContainerMixin,
    tkmix.ScheduleMixin, 
    tkmix.DestroyMixin, 
    tkmix.FocusMixin, 
    tkmix.ReprMixin,
    tkmix.SizeMixin
):
    def __init__(
                 self,
                 master,
                 title="guizero",
                 width=500,
                 height=500,
                 layout="auto",
                 bg=None
                ):
        self.tk = tk.Toplevel(master=master)

        self.description = "[Window] object"
        self.tk.title(str(title))
        self.tk.geometry(str(width)+"x"+str(height))
        self._layout_manager = layout

        if bg is not None:
            self.tk.configure(background=str(bg))

        self.tk.update()

    @property
    def title(self):
        return self.tk.title()

    @title.setter
    def title(self, text):
        self.tk.title(str(text))

    def display(self):
        self.tk.mainloop()

    def on_close(self, command):

No problem, happy to help - this is a great project you have. Those test
cases seem sensible, I'd also check that a plane has curvature zero.

One thing to note is that these are "curvature measures" produced by
averaging a curvature tensor across a mesh. The curvatures of a triangular
mesh are zero within the faces, but infinite on the edges (and vertices).
However, the integral over any region of non-zero measure is finite and
well defined. The region I have been using is the intersection of the mesh
with a ball of a given radius. To determine the curvatures, you should thus
divide by the area of this region, as in examples/curvature.ipynb. For an
arbitrary mesh, computing the area of this region exactly is
computationally intensive.

For a less computationally intensive approach which I would be happy to add
is to take the region to be the n-ring of a face. However, this wouldn't
produce accurate computations for irregular meshes.

and i'll try explain.

Hey @npezolano,

This is currently not supported out of the box. However, depending on what you need, you could do something like this:

```python
import bt
import ffn
import pandas as pd

data = bt.get('aapl')

s = bt.Strategy('s', [bt.algos.SelectAll(), 
                      bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                      bt.algos.Rebalance()])

# t1 to t2
t = bt.Backtest(s, data[:'2015-01-01'])
r = bt.run(t)

# t2 to t3
t2 = bt.Backtest(s, data['2015-01-01':])
r2 = bt.run(t2)
```

Now if you only need statistics like sharpe ratio and equity curve plots, then this should be enough:

```python
# extract returns to re-create whole equity curve
merged_returns = pd.concat([r.prices.to_returns(), r2.prices.to_returns()])
# calculate stats
stats = ffn.GroupStats(merged_returns.to_price_index())
```

With this, you will have most stats. However, you will not have weights and other such properties that are specific to Backtests. In this case, it would be more involved, but pretty much the same idea. You could create a new "merged Backtest" that contains the merged data from both Backtests (t.weights + t2.weights for example). You would then create a ```bt.backtest.Result``` by passing this new merged ```bt.Backtest``` into the ```bt.backtest.Result``` constructor.

Hope this helps,
Phil

Here we go!

py3's `round(0.5)` rounds towards the even choice (i.e. 0). py2's `round(0.5)` rounds away from 0 (i.e. 1). The py3 version is in accordance with the IEEE-754 specification default, R, and .NET, and removes sign bias as well as making the mean of a large set of rounded numbers the same as the mean if they were not rounded. 

The pythonic fix would be to make the py2 version depend on `future`, and then do `from builtins import round` (alternatively, use `np.round()`, as this has the new behaviour in both versions). This opens the door to writing more cross-compatible code in future, thus cutting down on effort required to maintain the two versions. It also means that using `2to3` to maintain the two versions is still viable.

However, this would be a departure from the behaviour of matlab, which rounds in a py2-like fashion.

It is also possible to make the py3 version depend on `past`, and use the builtins from that package. However, this change which would need to be checked every time the py3 codebase was regenerated with 2to3.

For completeness, and again AFAIU: the terminal apps set a locale depending on the region and language. If there is a mismatch between them (like me, setting English as language but Region as something else), the "UTF-8" locale is set as a fallback, and this one is OK for BSDs but not for GNU. And Python only understands GNU.

So only ...
* macOS users 
* with mismatched region and language 
* with a terminal app configured to automatically set the locale vars 

... will likely find this problem.

Setting locale should not break any other apps, and it helps with many. It’s kinda strange that macOS doesn’t set a correct one, but fixing it manually in shell config is simple.

Hey @ffeast, if you want to check if a complete element/record exists, you'll have to do a full search followed by an insert, if it doesn't exist. On the other hand, if you want to only check, if an element ID exists, you can use `db.contains(eids=[...])` to check, if that ID is already present.

In general, I think it would be worth considering if we should add a shorthand for the second option (if an ID exists). With regards to the first option, I think it will be better to do a regular search followed by an insert as it's a relatively expensive operation and the TinyDB API shouldn't pretend it anything else (`get_or_insert` would suggest a relatively cheap operation)

I see in your [prototype](https://github.com/marl/jams/pull/110/files) that you used rectangles for notes, and the y axis was set to a midi (numeric) scale.  I think this can be plugged directly into `labeled_intervals` as is.

I think we can do a little better if we define a [FuncFormatter](http://matplotlib.org/api/ticker_api.html#matplotlib.ticker.FuncFormatter) that can reinterpret midi numbers as pitches (with octave and cent deviation) or frequencies (Hz).  I'd prefer this to raw numbers or explicit ticker strings (like librosa currently does) because it will behave nicely with interactive plotting/zooming/etc.

For contours, we've talked about plotting on a cent axis.  I think it makes the most sense to just map to continuous/fractional midi numbers via `12 * (log2(f) - log2(440)) + 69`.  That way you can overlay pitch contours on quantized midi notes.

It would also be nice to plot contours directly in frequency space for easy overlaying on spectrograms.  Ultimately, this should be easy to do with a flag.  The only nontrivial part of pitch contour plotting then will be voicing detection and grouping of samples into fragments.

Boos python isn't going to be in PyPI  because it isn't directly useful
from Python.  It's a library used from C++ when you have C++ classes that
you want to make available to Python.  You might theoretically create some
module in PyPI by wrapping a native code library using Boost.Python.  I
once used it in an application I wrote to expose guts of the application to
an embedded Python script editor.  I wrote a blog post about some of that,
maybe skimming it will clarify for you more readily than digging through
all the boost docs:

https://willrosecrans.wordpress.com/2015/01/31/embedding-python-in-my-app-part-ii-the-desolation-of-documentation/

Because all of the Boost.Python classes show up as "built in" you won't be
able to have Jedi directly parse python source to figure out what makes up
a Boost Python class from first principles.  It would have to be done live
from introspection because it can all happen arbitrarily at run time in C++
code.

That's a tricky one: the path to the Python interpreter and nbstripout are indeed hard coded - deliberately. The point is to make the filter work independently of the environment where it is run (i.e. where you run `git`).

When using conda environments / virtualenv, nbstripout is installed with a prefix that may not be in the `$PATH` of your default shell environment. Hence using the filter in that environment would fail if we were just calling `nbstripout`.

Do you have a good idea for how to resolve this?

@jpeacock29 here's a mock example for PNG images: `$ nbstripout --regex='png": "i'`

That *should* get rid of the super long lines encoding PNG images for now.
I question the permanence of that regular expression since it is subject
to the formatting whims upstream. Historically, both `"image/png":`
and `"png":` have been used as keys, so my example would fortunately 
work for both cases. 

But --regex would be a handy tool in any case.

- Another option seems to be writing DTS overlays ([like on RPi](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33626480/raspberry-pi-gpio-pull-up-down-resistors-with-sysfs)) and rebooting each time. But it seems this is not yet supported (`/boot/dtb-4.13.8-sunxi/overlay/README.sun8i-h3-overlays`)?
- A similar Allwinner-specific solution are Fex Guide [GPIO configuration](http://linux-sunxi.org/Fex_Guide#Port_Definitions) and rebooting each time?

I tend to agree with @ratoaq2, there shouldn't be any logic in `guess_matches`, it should only parse tokens so that `compute_score` can do its job.

If property matches could be refactored to accept a dictionary of functions, where the key is the property name and the value is a function that accepts two values and return True/False, then subliminal could have a default function list to compute matches for each property, and through API it could allow a different dict to be used:

Something like this:
https://github.com/Diaoul/subliminal/blob/master/subliminal/subtitle.py#L199-L244
```
if match_functions['series'](video.series, guess.get('title')):
  matches.add('series')
if match_functions['title'](video.title, guess.get('episode_title')):
  matches.add('title')
if match_functions['season'](video.season, guess.get('season')):
  matches.add('season')
if match_functions['episode'](video.episode, guess.get('episode')):
  matches.add('episode')
if match_functions['year'](video.year, guess.get('year')):
  matches.add('year')
if match_functions['release_group'](video.release_group, guess.get('release_group')):
  matches.add('release_group')
```

That way it would be possible to tweak just part of the matching.



Another way to handle https://github.com/Diaoul/subliminal/issues/652#issuecomment-233577530
is to ensure that the subtitles are always sorted by:
- score
- by each property following the order of https://github.com/Diaoul/subliminal/blob/master/subliminal/score.py#L39-L43 where the absence of the property is ranked higher

For the case where there's no release_group and several subtitles score the same, the one with no release group will be first in the list:
```
score = 344 for castle.s08e22.crossfire.1080p.web-dl.dd5.1.h.264.srt (no release group)
score = 344 for castle.s08e22.1080p.web-dl.dd5.1.h264-btn.srt (btn)
score = 344 for castle.s08e22.crossfire.1080p.web.dl.dd5.1.h-ddltv.srt (ddltv)
score = 344 for castle.s08e22.crossfire.1080p.web-dl.dd5.1.h.264-hkd.srt (hkd)
score = 344 for castle.s08e22.crossfire.1080p.web-dl.dd5.1.hevc.x265-rmteam.srt  (rmteam)
```
So, if we scale down the max score because of the absence of a property, we can ensure that the first subtitle in the list is most likely the one we're searching for.

You would probably have to build a set of tags by iterating through each operation in each path of the ``paths`` key.

The [cache](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#variable-cache) is used to store the parsed column values. Within the cache, the actual row element is also stored and used to append to the tbody after the table is sorted or updated. You can access it like this:

```js
var config = table.config,
  tbodyIndex = 0, // zero-based index
  row = 10, // whatever row you want to access (zero-based index)
  dataIndex = config.columns, // extra data is stored after the last column of data
  data = config.cache[ tbodyIndex ].normalized[ row ][ dataIndex ];
```

The `data` element contains:

```js
{
  $row: <>,  // jQuery object of the entire table row
  child: [], // parsed child row data, if any
  order: #,  // the row order at initialization
  raw: []    // unparsed table cell textContent
}
```

So, you can modify an in-place row and it *will* update the cached `$row`; or access the `$row` within the cache... but the parsed cache (normalized) data won't get modified until you trigger an "update" on the table.

HIi @yotammanor,

Glad to hear that the Polyglot helps.

Polyglot relies on [ICU](http://site.icu-project.org/) for language detection, which is sometimes (e.g. for short text) not very accurate. In these cases (and in general any case where language is known), you can inform Text the language code using `hint_language_code` parameter.

Example is:

```python
>>> hebrew_text = u'זהו משפט בשפה העברית' #this is a sentence in Hebrew
>>> Text(hebrew_text, hint_language_code='he').language.code
'he'
```

Hmmm.  FFF has HTML clean up code that removes any empty or whitespace only(including ` `) tags except for `p` tags.  It also removes all except a few whitelisted tag attributes.

So here's what I've done in 7b34b7e5c2698c862f03dcc49475f5487ca8297e:

- I've added a new setting `keep_empty_tags` which defaults to `p,td,th`.
- I've added `colspan,rowspan` to the default value for `keep_html_attrs`.

That takes care of allowing empty table cells and row and column spans of same.

I have _**not**_ added attribute `style` to the default `keep_html_attrs`.  You can do that yourself by putting `add_to_keep_html_attrs:,style` in your personal.ini.  Put it under `[royalroadl.com]` for just that site or `[defaults]` for all sites.  Or use storyURL sections for specific stories.

What's the issue you are dealing with?

Currently ARouteServer does not support rpsl-via yet, even if it's on my [todo list](https://github.com/pierky/arouteserver/blob/master/docs/FUTUREWORK.rst).

Outbound announcements control (what a client wants to be announced to other clients) like the one achievable using rpsl-via can be implemented using the BGP communities offered by the tool, that allow to build a fine grained distribution matrix based on destination peer AS too (both using standard and extended/large communities for 32-bit ASN support).

Inbound announcements control can't be achieved with the options currently offered by ARouteServer; a client must implement local filters to accept or reject routes sent by the route server. This is the point where, IMHO, this feature could be more useful.

Back to the AMS-IX example, a very quick and dirty research I made showed that 81 over 675 route server participants have at least an "import-via/export-via AS6777" entry in their aut-num object, that is 12% (please take these numbers with a grain of salt).

It's definitely a feature that's worth to be added to ARouteServer. It seems I must give a higher priority to it, even if I guess it will take some time before having it ready.

I have set up a routine which outputs a model's .paramnames file. The paramnames file is a file which lists every model parameter and its latex label. It is set up using ClassMap. As shown in the test case, if a ClassMap comprises two models the paramnames file will look as follows:

0_profile_centre_0                           $x$
0_profile_centre_1                           $y$
0_profile_axis_ratio                         $q$
0_profile_phi                                    $\phi$
1_mass_profile_centre_0                $x_{\mathrm{d}}$
1_mass_profile_centre_1                $y_{\mathrm{d}}$
1_mass_profile_kappa_s                 $\kappa_{\mathrm{d}}$
1_mass_profile_scale_radius          $Rs_{\mathrm{d}}$

The paramnames file is a default file used by MultiNest, for assigning parameter names and latex labelling axes.

This means there are two types of plots we will want after an analysis:

1) Plots which only require the paramnames file and therefore do not use the ClassMap routine to set up the mode in AuotLens. For example, plots of parameter confidences (1D and 2D). Therefore, I will begin by writing plotting routines for these files which are a separate layer from the ClassMap / Galaxy classes.

However, we may still want to tie these plots to ClassMap at some level, if say we wanted to plot just the confidences for a specific Galaxy, or a specific MassProfile over a set of images. It should be possible to call the routines I write above from the Galaxy class with specific inputs that give the desired behaviour (e.g. the MultiNest plotting routines could take specific parameter indexes which only the Galaxy classes 'knows' about).

2) Plots which require us to setup a model via ClassMap and run them through AutoLens. This includes anything which actually analyses the image (residuals, chi^2 image, source reconstructions, mass profiles, light profiles, etc.) and plots which arn't used in the analysis but are tied to the light or mass profile (e.g. surface density, potential, mass / luminosity integrals)/ 

To facilitate this, I've written routines which from the MultiNest output load a set of results (e.g. the best-fit model, most probable model, models corresponding to upper / lower error limits) and then use the ClassMap routine to set this up as a model. The model can then be passed through the normal AutoLens routines as per usual.

I had exactly the same problem you mention regarding testing maths functions.
I've found the best way is to test the basic logical case for rotations, i.e. unit X rotate 1/2pi == -unit Z.

For more complex situations I've had to resort to wolfram alpha which can give some very good, specific, answers to questions.
Having a quick look, it doesn't seem to understand 'matrix look at' or similar search queries =/.

I think the best is still what I mentioned previously.
Create a look at matrix which looks, say, right (1/2pi rotation clockwise).
Apply to a unit x, unit y, and unit z vector.
Check that the unit vectors now face unit z, unit y, unit -x, respectively.
Then duplicate this for look up, etc.

From PyEuclid (https://code.google.com/p/pyeuclid/source/browse/trunk/euclid.py)

    def new_look_at(cls, eye, at, up):
      z = (eye - at).normalized()
      x = up.cross(z).normalized()
      y = z.cross(x)
      
      m = cls.new_rotate_triple_axis(x, y, z)
      m.d, m.h, m.l = eye.x, eye.y, eye.z
      return m

    def new_rotate_triple_axis(cls, x, y, z):
      m = cls()
      
      m.a, m.b, m.c = x.x, y.x, z.x
      m.e, m.f, m.g = x.y, y.y, z.y
      m.i, m.j, m.k = x.z, y.z, z.z
      
      return m

Using this code, we could create a matrix then compare against the result of pyrr's equivalent look at function.

I've noticed that there is a difference in how the X,Y,Z are computed.
Ours does `target - eye`, and PyEuclid does `eye - at`.
Perhaps the function accounts for this, as ours uses `-np.dot(...)` and theirs doesn't.
I think this shows that we need to do the unit x,y,z rotation to prove the function.
-Z is camera forward (right-handed coords), so applying a look at matrix to a vector will resolve this.

I should probably add that 'matrix from 3 vectors' function too =P.

If you look at my commits you'll see that the first quick fix i came up with for this issue, was to simply wrap the `change` event handlers call to `validate` in a $timeout. Which then safely calls `scope.$apply`. Which is essentially what you're doing. So i understand why the issue is occurring.

The problem i have with the quick fix of `scope.$apply`, is that *it* feels like a hack. Its just a brute force solution that fails to understand why this issue only occurs in FF and not in chrome, safari or IE.

That aside, i can understand why you might consider my solution using `ng-change` to be a 'hack', as you believe it accidentally fixes the issue. But i can assure you it was a very much intentional and considered approach. 

Within angular an inputs current state is represented by its `ngModel`. But your logic mixes and matches angular model/controller state and DOM state (checkbox.checked) to determine the current state of the form. Which is not the correct way to handle this scenario. You should be monitoring the checkbox's `ngModel` for change (thus the use of the `ng-change` directive) and then use those values to determine the current state of the form. The `ngModel` should be the single source of state.

Regardless of how `ng-change` is added to the input (i'm currently looking at an alternative solution that uses `ngModel.$viewlisteners` instead - **but am also wondering if this attribute couldn't just be added in the python widget?**), i strongly believe that what i've outlined and initially implemented, is the correct way to deal with this scenario.
Also, seing PR #210 we could use dict comprehension instead of `dict()`. Py2.7 is a standard almost everywhere and we do not maintain previous version of python.

So if we're convinced that `minidom` is the problem, switching to `lxml` is probably the best course of action.  Doing so will not be easy, though.  The original code base was generated using `generateDS`, how it got setup originally is [described here](https://breathe.readthedocs.io/en/latest/codeguide.html#doing-the-parsing), though I'm not sure what files were used to generate it in the first place.  Based on the current parser setup, I *believe* you only need to use `index.xsd` and `compound.xsd`.  You can obtain those by just generated Doxygen XML documentation once, they'll show up next to the other XML documents (e.g. right next to `index.xml`).


I **think** you may be able to regenerate using `lxml`, but then you'd need to crawl through the diffs and figure out what changed.  The [`generateDS` docs say it requires `lxml`](http://www.davekuhlman.org/generateDS.html#requirements), that may not have been the case when breathe was first created.  It's not clear to me what the answer is, but I think there is a way to possibly have both available in the same code base.  [I'm looking here](http://www.davekuhlman.org/generateDS.html#generating-lxml-element-tree).  But I also think that requires that breathe had been generated using `export`, which I don't think is true.

Myself, I would vote for (if it actually works), replacing `minidom` with `lxml` entirely.  If I remember correctly, `minidom` is pure python, whereas `lxml` has a C backend (that is a belief, not a fact).

So to do it, I think the steps you wold need to take are

1. Regenerate the super mod stuff with `generateDS`, making sure that the `lxml` stuff actually gets created.  There's likely more than just setting `export` to `etree`, you'd have to read the docs there more carefully.
2. Assuming you get something very similar to the existing parser package, you would then need to make sure the [changes](https://breathe.readthedocs.io/en/latest/codeguide.html#our-changes) get spliced back in.

Then I think all that changes is this method to use `lxml` instead?

https://github.com/michaeljones/breathe/blob/08577a4b7f9a9173517d7ddf91bd1fa50eac1252/breathe/parser/index.py#L47-L60

I don't know if the `parser` package can just be replaced though, there's likely a lot more to it :/

A few notes:
* I removed the ability to share form the PlexLibrary object. The plex.tv API requires that we always send all the libraries we want to share (if its not included, it will be removed as a shared library). So if you tried to share a library it would remove all other shares for that user. I could have looked them up and append, but it would be more work than it was worth and require we run 3 or 4 queries just to get the information needed, then it got even more complicated when adding filters to a share.
* Also, my current thinking is that all communication to plex.tv should happen in the myplex.py module. It provides a clean way of thinking about the library and who is communicating with what.
Its prop because of https://github.com/pkkid/python-plexapi/blob/61ede66ad5e2d0d21cf9dd5895226b263b67a1d5/plexapi/client.py#L449

You found a bug. Python ignores 'private' variables (e.g. `_type`) when importing into the current namespace, which is how lackey/sikuli are typically used. From [PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#descriptive-naming-styles):

> In addition, the following special forms using leading or trailing underscores are recognized (these can generally be combined with any case convention):
> 
> `_single_leading_underscore`: weak "internal use" indicator. E.g. `from M import *` does not import objects whose name starts with an underscore.

```python
# Python 3.6.1 (v3.6.1:69c0db5, Mar 21 2017, 17:54:52) [MSC v.1900 32 bit (Intel)] on win32
>>> type('test')

>>> from lackey import *
>>> type('test')
[action] Typing 'test' with modifiers 'None'
>>> _type('test')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
NameError: name '_type' is not defined
```
It works with importing in its own namespace:

```python
>>> type('test')
<class 'str'>
>>> import lackey
>>> lackey.type('test')
[action] Typing 'test' with modifiers 'None'
>>> lackey._type('test')
<class 'str'>
```

Changing `_type` to `type_` (and `_input` to`input_`) fixes this bug:

```python
>>> type('test')
<class 'str'>
>>> from lackey import *
>>> type('test')
[action] Typing 'test' with modifiers 'None'
>>> type_('test')
<class 'str'>
```

To keep backwards compatibility (if someone has used this in the past by providing a namespace with e.g. `import lackey`), both options would have to be available. I can submit a PR later if this is an acceptable solution?

> It looks like you could try ... `pkgutil`

Yes, and I would if it weren't for the impossible compatibility issues. Note the warning in the packaging guide: 

> While native namespace packages and pkgutil-style namespace packages are largely compatible, pkg_resources-style namespace packages are not compatible with the other methods. It’s inadvisable to use different methods in different distributions that provide packages to the same namespace.

What that means is that if I switch the `jaraco` namespace to use `pkgutil` to extend the packages, I need to do it for all `jaraco*` packages and any environment that upgrades even a single one of those packages needs to upgrade all of them.

And while there are only a few packages in the public domain that rely on more than one of these, there are probably hundreds, many of which are nested and interdependent, in my company's projects.

So I've not spent the effort to transition all of those packages. If it could be shown that using pkgutil would solve most or all of these compatibility issues, that would be a strong incentive to make the switch. But otherwise, I'm more inclined to let `jaraco` (and `yg` for that matter) remain on the legacy pkg_resources-managed approach until the projects drop support for Python 3.3 and 2.7, which I expect will happen in the coming year.

I'm not familiar with robocopy, but from what I've been able to find on it, I think the answer is no.

As I understand it, robocopy gets you:
* recursive copies of directories
* some sort of network copy (it appears to include bandwidth limiting)
* the ability to resume from interrupted transfers
* skipping target files that appear identical (same size+timestamps)
* delete target files that were removed from the source

Magic-wormhole only gets you one-shot copies of a file or directory. You could wrap it a cron job to get repeated copies, but it will refuse to overwrite target files that already exist (unless you override that), and it makes no attempt to utilize partial data that's already at the destination. It won't delete anything.

Robocopy sounds a lot like `rsync` in the unix world, and `rsync` also does a lot of clever things to minimize the bandwidth used when the target already looks mostly like the source. To use `rsync` securely, you need to run it over `ssh`, for which you need a public key on the far end (but magic-wormhole is a great way to deliver that pubkey: check out `wormhole ssh accept` and `wormhole ssh invite`).

And neither approach protects you against the backup host seeing your files. For client-side encryption of backups, take a look at https://tahoe-lafs.org (I'm also an author).

Hope that answers your question!

Every time your master branch changes, build.snapcraft.io will build a new snap and release it to the edge channel.

To simplify the packaging tasks, I prefer to use [git annotated tags](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4971746/why-should-i-care-about-lightweight-vs-annotated-tags) (which is a good practice anyway IMO). With that, you can set `version: git` in the snapcraft.yaml. And the next time you make a release tag in github, that version will land properly identified in edge. If you want that version to be visible on user searches, and to go to all your stable users, you use the `snapcraft release` to put it in the stable channel.

We hang in https://rocket.ubuntu.com and https://forum.snapcraft.io, in case you have more questions or need a hand with something.

After several datatype bugfixes, this issue seems to have been partially resolved, as calling record(0) on the qs_localities file now works without causing an error, as does iterating through every single record. 

However, I also got those strange values with offsets and asterixes. It is not a matter of a corrupt file since eg QGIS reads the values fine without all the weirdness. Interestingly, those fields you mention contain text and should therefore not be type "N" (numeric). According to QGIS the file only contains "S" string and "I" integer fields, whereas PyShp wrongly interprets some of them as "N" numeric, which is probably the likely culprit, reading the wrong number of bytes for each value. 

So this definitively deserves a closer inspection at some point, and PyShp's parsing of the dbf fieldtypes in the header is probably a good place to start.

Rather than failing out of the installation on Python 3, why not make the package empty when it's installed on Python 3, so installation will 'succeed' but won't shadow the standard library? Something like this:

```python
packages=['concurrent', 'concurrent.futures'] if sys.version_info == 2 else [],
```

If this behavior is ever proposed upstream, my bet is that core Python developers will reject it *in limine*. 

* The whole point of an **asynchronous** framework like `concurrent.futures` is to **never** block. 
* The patch proposed in this PR is unacceptable, you cannot just naively busy-loop waiting for item in the pending work to be consumed, that's not the way we do multithreading in Python!
* If memory pressure is an issue, this dirty fix proposed here is rather trivial apply by either subclassing or monkeypatching. But an **asynchronous** framework should not trade memory pressure for blocking behavior.

This might be outdated, but just in case anyone else is wondering about another way to do this without patching django-social-auth, here's my take on it:

The authentication process sends a request to the authorization provider along with a URL the provider should redirect to when the authentication is complete. By default, that URL is ```complete/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/``` (found in django-social-auth's ```urls.py```). However, looking at the code for the authentication process (in ```views.auth```, which calls ```views.auth_process```), you'll see that the view looks for a ```REDIRECT_FIELD_NAME``` (a constant for *next*, from django.contrib.auth) in the request parameters. That allows you to specify a different URL to redirect to once the authentication is done.

In your case, I would create two views to be redirected to and add URL mappings for them in your configuration. One for regular users and one for your expert users. In each registration form, I'd have the *next* parameter point to new view's URL. This way, when the redirect comes in, you know which type of user has just authenticated with the third party.

As for the content of the views, a quick look in ```views.complete``` shows that it calls ```views.complete_process``` directly. You can do the same and once it returns, trigger the appropriate signal.

As a shortcut, you could also use a single view and map it to two URLs, each providing a kwarg to indicate what kind of user is registering.

the reason that simple-encrypt is slow is that it uses a PBKDF to help make "poor" passwords more secure.  in short, anyone trying to guess your password will also need to spend 3-5 second per guess.

this is acceptable (imho) when used just once or twice by a program (eg to read or save some results in an encrypted way).  if you need something that does many encryptions / decryptions every time your program runs then you probably need to use a different library.

sorry - that's just the trade-off in involved.  simple-crypt tries to be safe, perhaps at the expense of being efficient.

You can implement signal-based timeout using `contextlib2.ContextDecorator`.  Something like this:

```
class signal_timeout(ContextDecorator):
    """
    Enforces timeout via signals.  Can be applied as a context manager or decorator.
    """
    class NotInstalled(object): pass

    def __init__(self, seconds):
        """
        :type seconds: int|float
        :param seconds: Number of seconds before timeout occurs.
        """
        self.seconds = seconds
        self._prev_signal_handler = self.NotInstalled

    def __enter__(self):
        if self.seconds > 0:
            self._prev_signal_handler = \
                signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, self._handle_timeout)
            signal.setitimer(signal.ITIMER_REAL, self.seconds)

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        if self._prev_signal_handler is not self.NotInstalled:
            signal.setitimer(signal.ITIMER_REAL, 0)
            signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, self._prev_signal_handler)
            self._prev_signal_handler = self.NotInstalled

    @staticmethod
    def _handle_timeout(signal_no, stack_trace):
        raise TimeoutError()
```

With _ccu <-> device_ you mean the communication between the CCU and a thermostat or whatever? If so, the CCU has the "Servicemeldungen". These aren't directly visible in HASS (yet, it would be possible though if someon would implement it), but the ccu-entity within HASS always has a numeric value, which corresponds to the total count of such service messages. So if it's always 0 and then turns to 1 you know, that at least some sort of error has occurred. That way you know you should have a look at the CCU and check which error has happened.

The _ccu <-> hass_ connection is hard to monitor. A dropped connection usually results in your hass-devices not updating anymore, since the CCU stops sending the event-data to hass. But the CCU does not send a "we had a dropped connection"-message. And the CCU also lacks som sort of "ping"-method which we could use to check if the connection is still alive. So in that case you only have the workaround to have an automation that fires as soon as some sensor hasn't changed for time X. A brightness-sensor for example which is configured to periodically send the brightness. Such a sensor should change its value a few times across a whole if it's near a window. Or something similar, like a sensor measuring the power usage of the house, which never is 0W if you have a CCU running.

The sensor does not exist in the UI at the moment - it's just like the HmIP wall switches, which only appear as keypress events when someone pressed a button.

> Just tested this with our setup and it doesn't work yet. If the program with the lock crashes and you run it again before the renewal runs out, it will block forever here because the expire is 0. 

I don't see how this could be the case, unless you explicitly set expire to 0/None, in which case having a renewal doesn't make sense. 

The call to [self._client.set()](https://github.com/zoni/python-redis-lock/blob/refresh_interval/src/redis_lock/__init__.py#L90) definitely should ***not*** have `ex=self._expire or self._lock_renewal_interval` but should remain at `ex=self._expire` because it sets the expire time for the lock, in no case should it set the renew interval as the expiry time.

We could however consider enforcing that an `expire` time is supplied when `renewal` is given, as it generally doesn't make sense to set the latter without the former.

Thanks for the PR! It seems like there's demand for this feature, so it's definitely worth considering. A few notes:

1. The solution should be configurable and default to the current behavior to preserve backward compatibility.

2. `pytz` is a bit of a heavy dependency to add just for this purpose. Python 3.2+ includes [`datetime.timezone.utc`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.timezone.utc). The Python 2 [`tzinfo` documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/datetime.html#tzinfo-objects) includes an example UTC tzinfo class definition. It seems like using these (possibly in the 2-to-3 compatibility sections of `utils.py`) would be sufficient to fix the issue.

3. If a third party solution is used, its requirement should not be pinned to a specific version. With the case of `pytz` (and any other library that tracks timezone information), you likely always want the most up-to-date version.

4. `pytz` includes all (500+) of the Olson timezone files in its distribution. This is unfortunately a problem on Google AppEngine (which has a very small file limit), leading to libraries like [gaerpytz](https://gaerpytz.appspot.com/) as workarounds. Adding pytz as a Flask-Security dependency would break applications being deployed on GAE, which is undesirable.

TL;DR: the solution here is probably configurable behavior to use a UTC `tzinfo` implementation taken directly from the Python docs rather than depending on another library.

There is a lot of overlap between the two libraries. `pandas-summary` has a `_get_summary_` method for all column types, while `pandas-profiling` has a `describe `method for each variable type. Other than that, I think the code is fairly similar for calculating stats etc. 

In `pandas-profiling` however, some stats are missing like MAD deviation. It also returns a dictionary, and not a clean object that is queryable like `pandas-summary`.

I think the best approach would be to merge the `_get_summary` methods with the `_describe` methods, and then add the property methods and `__getitem__` methods to `pandas-profiling` base class. Lowest effort would be to leave the internal data structure of `pandas-profiling` intact (dictionary based), otherwise a lot of refactoring will need to happen. `pandas-summary` does not store data, only calculates in on-demand but the whole idea would be to calculate once and then re-use the statistics both in an object and in an HTML report.

The database is used to store a list of dependencies for the compiled files. This is necessary to recompile the main file when one of the imported files were changed. If you don't need dependency tracking then you can create your custom LESS compiler like so:

```
class LESS(static_precompiler.compilers.less.LESS):
     supports_dependencies = False
```

and register it in your project's settings:

```
STATIC_PRECOMPILER_COMPILERS = (
    'custom_compiler.LESS',
)
 ```

There's one problem with that, though. I just looked through the code and found one place where the `supports_dependencies` attribute is not taken into account: https://github.com/andreyfedoseev/django-static-precompiler/blob/master/static_precompiler/compilers/base.py#L313

As a workaround for that you can override `get_dependents` method in your custom compiler to make it return an empty list.

@mpauly 

Sorry for the late reply. I understand your use case now but I still hesitate over the proposed solution. 

Would the following work for you:

1. Add underscore prefix to the files that are impored in `bootstrap.less`: `_alerts.less`, `_buttons.less` and so on.
2. Update `@import` directives `bootstrap.less` to include underscores

This would prevent static precompiler from compiling those files when the management command is run.

This approach is very common in SASS/SCSS (see http://sass-lang.com/documentation/file.SASS_REFERENCE.html#Partials__partials). If you used SASS/SCSS the second step (updating `bootstrap.scss`) wouldn't be necessary.

Even though partials aren't implemented in Less (see see https://github.com/less/less.js/issues/2295), static precompiler respects the underscore prefix in `.less` file names and doesn't try compile them when the management command is run.

I don't have a hands-on experience on audio-visual signals, so you might want to consult other researchers who do. 

First, if your the image/video representation is *unrelated to human speech* (e.g. musical instruments, objects sounds, etc), you might want to use STFT instead of a **vocoder** (e.g. WORLD/STRIGHT) because the latter distorts or even ignores speech-unrelated signals. (You can verify this by analyzing and then synthesizing a singing song with music.)

Second, you can adjust the frame rate to match the video's fps. At least two choices are available:
  1) set `frame_period=1000/30`.
  2) set `frame_period` to smaller values (say, `1000/240`), and then down-sample the output speech feature to 30 fps (8x in this case; using either standard DSP or a CNN).  

Make sure that the output is properly aligned because sometimes non-integer frame rate may give you additional frame(s) (either in WORLD or in STFT. Do whatever is needed to deal with it. Also note that some video coding could sometimes unexpectedly give you extra frames.

Hope you find it useful and wish you the best for your research.

Thanks for the question.

`new_kt_to_tensor` doesn't work because, as the hint suggests, indexed assignment into arrays isn't supported in autograd. (`kt_to_tensor` probably also won't work because it uses in-place updating via `+=`, though it wasn't getting to that error.) See [the tutorial](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/docs/tutorial.md) for more information, particularly the "don't use" list.

I think the original code is using `np.ix_` to implement broadcasting, and it's probably better to use more standard broadcasting constructs here, as [the StackOverflow answer](http://stackoverflow.com/a/43253852) suggests, or to use `np.einsum`.

I'm going to close this issue because it seems that your problem is solved. A PR to implement `np.ix_` support is welcome, though!

Hallo @stefankoegl 

I share your view that 1.0 should imply a stable interface rather that feature completeness and as such I think we are a good deal along the way towards that goal. In my view there are mainly three things that would block this:

1. **Radio station and show handling:** Right now those are still returned as dicts instead of using a data structure. As far as I can tell, that is the last piece to the puzzle to make handling of media work by means of the data structures. I actually already have a branch lying around for it, but it needs a little more polishing
2. **Album art uris for music library items:** There has previously been some debate about how to handle those. Remember that the issue is that the album art uri you get when you query data structures is not complete. You need to prefix it with the address of a Sonos unit. In the earlier discussions I argued that the absolute uri's should only be formed in request by a Sonos instance (the current implementation), to make sure that the data structures are completely serializable. However, since we had those discussions I am starting to think that I was wrong. Maybe it would be better if the data structures actually held a reference to the soco instance that created them. This would mean that the data structures could return a complete working uri without having to go through a SoCo instance. The price would be, that you would then have to pass in a reference to the SoCo instance that should be the owner when you deserialize. All in all I'm actually starting to lean towards this being the better solution (also since I think there are more clients that need the ). In any case, that would be an interface breaking change.
3. **New data structure maturity:** It seems (@lawrenceakka correct my if I am wrong) that there is still perhaps a bit more work to do with the data structures that has to do with Sonos not always following the spec. This manifests itself in error when using the event code. So I think a bit more time and testing with this might be good before we declare the data structures stable.

On a completely other note, besides a stable interface, I was thinking that it might also be a good idea to make it a goal to have complete docs on the features available and complete test coverage with at least unit tests before we call it 1.0.

Regards Kenneth

I think this goes along with [the Robustness_principle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robustness_principle).

The client needs to be strict about what it sends the server, but it should be liberal about what it accepts. Extra fields that it doesn't know about are ok, it can just ignore them.

One solution might be to add a new config value to bravado_core, which would enable this behaviour, and we could default it to True for the client.

I don’t think the jr instruction has anything to do with it - NoDecode
means that libVEX couldn’t handle the bytecode and errored out instead of
producing any IR, and it has definitely produced IR for that whole basic
block. The real culprit will be a block that has jumpkind Ijk_NoDecode, and
the instruction causing the issue will be the one right after the end of
the block.

Thanks for the explanation, I see now that it is correct!

> I'm not sure if I understand it. Can you please elaborate on this?

The problems arise when we reach the `call_depth` specified. 

Lets look at this simple example:
```
A ---call---> B ---ret---> C
 \--------fakeret---------^
```
If we have reached the call depth, then we don't want to trace into B, instead we want to continue tracing with node C. 

This code was part of the DDG once (now commented out, but still used in VSA_DDG) to select the successors to continue the tracing with.
```python
corresponding_successors = [n for n in successing_nodes if not state.ip.symbolic and n.addr == state.se.any_int(state.ip)]
```
The problem with this is, that in the case above  C will not be in corresponding_successors, since A's final state "points to" B.

What the code does currently is to follow all existing successors for every final state. This is also not correct but it helps in this case, since we will follow the fakeret edge, so tracing into C will happen. As we agreed following the fakeret edge is usually a bug, but when the call depth is reached, actually this is what we should do.

**So I think what we need is the clever combination of this two logics**

What I thought (originally) when I wrote the previous comment (**I see now that this is not needed!**):
In `binary_optimizer.py` the analysis is initialized with a cfg. Then later for every function it builds a new cfg like this (call_depth=1, +using the original cfg/kb as a base):
```python
cfg = self.project.analyses.CFGAccurate(kb=func_kb, call_depth=1,base_graph=function.graph,keep_state=True,starts=(function.addr,),iropt_level=0)
```
So I thought we could "rebuild" the CFG, with the corresponding `call_depth` parameter, using the original CFG as a base. Then we could use this rebuilt CFG to help the traversal.

So, this has less to do with the actual endness of memory and more to do with the endness of the instruction format.  We copied all of the instruction formats out of the MSP430 ISA as-written, only to find that the actual instructions were stored in memory endy-flipped! Gah! 
We want people to be able to describe the format as naturally as possible, so being able to correct for this at the lifter is pretty convenient.  The only question is where that option should be stored

That may be some minor part of the more major question about the future of archinfo... but for now, will an elegant hack work? Like "instruction_endness"? (which defaults to something sane)

Hi @JordonPhillips 

> You would need to register your own handler that adds it in (just make sure to register it before add_glacier_checksums, or to remove the handler entirely)

I've tried the proposed solution, and it's certainly not elegant but it kind of works.  _Unfortunately, the names and types of the arguments have already been validated before the first event handler gets called._  Therefore it does not allow us to specify additional parameters in the request, an exception will be raised before we reach the event handler.  Fortunately the length of the checksum is not validated, so I was able to hack in this workaround:

```

def my_event_handler(params, **kwargs):
    headers = params['headers']
    headers['x-amz-sha256-tree-hash'], headers['x-amz-content-sha256'] = headers['x-amz-sha256-tree-hash'].split()

client.meta.events.register_first('before-call.glacier.UploadMultipartPart', my_event_handler)

# Then we can do:
multipart_upload_instance.upload_part(
    range=byte_range,
    body=BytesIO(data),
    checksum=' '.join([tree_hash, payload_hash]),
)

```

I leave this here in case it helps other users to workaround this issue until the glacier service definitions can be updated.

No, this is not trivial because of DyNet's memory management scheme.

One common request I get is that people want LookupParameters where we don't know the vocabulary size before hand. In this case a current work-around is to create a LookupParameter object with a large vocabulary that will definitely be bigger than your expected vocabulary size.

If we want to fix this issue, I see two options:
1. We could do something tricky within the current memory management paradigm, but I'm not sure what.
2. We could write an additional memory manager that supports deletion of memory blocks. This wouldn't be too hard, and would allow us to allocate a new memory block, copy the parameters, then delete the old memory block.

Currently, if you deploy an app using any other scheduler in Deis, that app has env vars that pertain to itself.

The k8s preview, though, sets 7 environment variables for _every single app_ that is deployed in Deis. Every single app gets information on how to connect to every other app throughout Deis.

To make k8s work the way current Deis works, we should give each app its own namespace so that it will (by default) only know about itself.

In the future, I think we can do some fantastic things with the namespacing feature. But for the preview, I feel like we should stay as close to the current Deis scheme as we can.

@krancour You should probably be the owner of this ticket now, since I'm way out in other-stuff-world right now, and you're way more familiar than I am.

I can see where this would be an added convenience at the time a cluster is initially provisioned, _but_ one of the primary motivations for running a stateless Deis cluster is that with the state persisted _outside_ the cluster, it's easy to rebuild or replace a cluster without _losing_ state.  If management of the RDS database were coupled to the management of the cluster itself, it becomes impossible to tear down the cluster without also tearing down the database.  Ultimately, that would negate the benefits of running a stateless cluster.

In my own experience managing Deis clusters on AWS (before becoming a Deis maintainer), I tried to always look at my clusters as a layered compositions of CloudFormation stacks.  I created separate CloudFormation templates for managing the VPC (into which I would potentially deploy multiple clusters), and cluster state, which included an RDS database and an S3 bucket for use by the registry.

The VPC stack was "foundational."  Conceptually, other stacks (some unrelated to Deis) sat atop it.  Then for each Deis cluster, I created the "state stack" from the aforementioned template and ran the Deis provisioning scripts to create the stack that manages the Deis cluster itself.  This allowed me to peel back and replace that "topmost" layer at any time without affecting anything "underneath" it (like platform state).

In general, I understand this sort of careful layering to be a good practice with respect to CloudFormation.  I'm not sure I could get on board with explicitly defying that within our provisioning process.

> This variable shall represent a pathname of the user's preferred command language interpreter.

That's all it says (plus a note about behaviour of "utilities" being undefined when `$SHELL` isn't POSIX-compatible). Fish is certainly your "preferred command language interpreter", so afaict it's totally valid for `$SHELL` to point to Fish.

To install private github repository from pip the syntax is
`pip install git+ssh://git@github.com/username/repo.git@branch#egg=blah`

This worked properly with pipenv up to pipenv 10
`pipenv install git+ssh://git@github.com/username/repo.git@branch#egg=blah`

but with pipenv 11 
`pipenv install git+ssh://git@github.com/username/repo.git@branch#egg=blah`
returns with the an error for
`git clone -q git@github.com/username/repo.git /tmp/temp`
it's because the correct syntax should be `git clone -q git@github.com:username/repo.git /tmp/temp` with `:` and not `/`

I'm taking a look at this, I'm probably going to end up pulling it in in pieces so I can make sure I fully understand all the parts.

Some points:

1. If virtualenv writes a pyvenv.cfg, it should probably identify itself somehow (e.g., with a "creator=virtualenv" entry).
2. You'd need to confirm that Python wouldn't get confused if a pyvenv.cfg was written by something other than venv. Does the mere existence of pyvenv.cfg cause Python to behave differently?
3. I'm not at all sure I'm in favour of the motivating use case in the referenced issue (which means adding more special casing to the shebang handling in the launcher). A better solution might be to enable path searching for ```#!/usr/bin/env pythonX.Y``` and modify virtualenv and venv to write ```pythonX.exe``` and ```pythonX.Y.exe``` executables as well as ```python.exe```. (There has been talk previously of the Windows installer providing ```pythonX.exe``` and ```python X.Y.exe``` for Unix compatibility, so extending that model to virtualenvs is not unreasonable). Or for that matter simply not doing anything, as it's not a particularly common issue (given the lack of bug reports or requests about it).

e2e flow for create a linux based webapp
```bash
az appservice list-locations --linux-workers-enabled --sku S1 #check which regions for linux
az resource group  -n yugangwlinux2 -l "Southeast Asia"
az resource group create  -n yugangwlinux2 -l "Southeast Asia" #known limitation that a group should never contain any windows webapps
az appservice plan create -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangwlinux2-plan --sku s1 --is-linux

#option 1, configure a docker container based webapp
az appservice web create -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangw-webapp-container --plan yugangwlinux2-plan
az appservice web config container update -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangw-webapp-container --docker-custom-image-name "naziml/ruby-hello"
#launches the browser, need to wait a few minutes for server side configured and return a text of "Hello World from Ruby!"
az appservice web browse -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangw-webapp-container

#option 2, configure a non docker based based webapp
az appservice web create -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangw-webapp-regular --plan yugangwlinux2-plan
#enable git, copy the url and use it to clone
az appservice web git enable-local -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangw-webapp-regular
#develop your linux webs
#unzip attached file, push all files to git ....
az appservice web config update -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangw-webapp-regular  --app-command-line process.json
#launches the browser, and see your page gets loaded
az appservice web browse -g yugangwlinux2 -n yugangw-webapp-regular





```

For some reason, pip on WebApp didn't choose to download the wheel file for cryptography on PyPI. I guess the introspection of pip wasn't able to get the "win32" or "win_amd64" tags on this system.

I suggest you to try the options on this document:

IOW, either download [cryptography-1.5-cp34-cp34m-win32.whl](https://pypi.python.org/packages/83/5b/59b7de8c938a1856585d91cccd648c3ff2af14ec518051b3bfd50d6a9f73/cryptography-1.5-cp34-cp34m-win32.whl#md5=896b2588347b6d2ebdba2754b035ed1a), put it in a "wheelhouse" folder and use `--find-links` or a deployment script using `easy_install`

As the previous document says, you will not be able to compile cryptography directly on the WebApp server, so you need a way to force the install of the wheel file.

I hope it helps,
Also running into this issue.

I think a good way to resolve this would be to pull azure-sdk-for-python's name generation up into azure-python-devtools.

The SDK gets resource names by taking a name prefix and adding a checksum based on the fully qualified test name (i.e. `module_name.test_name`).
The qualified test name is determined here: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/v2.0.0rc6/azure-common/testutils/common_recordingtestcase.py#L51
and it's combined with the checksum here: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/v2.0.0rc6/azure-common/testutils/common_recordingtestcase.py#L97

This should be a general enough approach to go in azure-devtools, and as @lmazuel pointed out it has the additional advantage of allowing the user to change the order in which resources are created without breaking tests. The CLI's "count resources" approach does not have that property.

Brainstorming here, one possible variant on #988 might be requiring channels to be type-homogenous. Then, if you depend on a uuid package of type 'R', then it must come from a channel of type 'R'. However, what happens then if you run `conda install uuid` and have two channels of different type that conda could install from?
* Conda responds that this is ambiguous and requires you to pass --channel or --type. This is an annoying, if safe, UI for a single package.
* Your conda environment also has a type and will only look in channels of that type. I can't quite put my finger on why this seems like the wrong way to do it.
* Your conda environment has a default type but and will look at channel of that type first and then fall back to other channel types. This fails silently and dangerously if you think a package in the default type exists but it does not.

That channel `https://conda.binstar.org/rsignell` uses an outdated URL.
`binstar.org` moved to `anaconda.org` a while back.
You can remove that channel by running
```
conda config --remove channels 'https://conda.binstar.org/rsignell'
```
*If* you really need this channel, you can add it back (after removing it with the command above) via
```
conda config --add channels rsignell
```
which will automatically use the newer `conda.anaconda.org` domain.

----
For reference why this may not have happened before:
```
conda.binstar.org uses an invalid security certificate. The certificate expired on April 21, 2018, 1:59 AM. The current time is June 2, 2018, 5:36 PM.
```

This metadata is changed in our index. We sometimes make mistakes when we create packages. This was one of them. There original constraint was far too restrictive. If we change the package, though, the hashsum of the package changes. 

Not an issue, other than if you build your own local collection of packages somehow, you'll only have the original metadata that might be broken or not ideal. There will be a way for local indexes the patch metadata someone soon.

**Sourcing** is the name of the game.

Sourcing and executing scripts are very different animals.

This goes into extensive discussion concerning this: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29832037/how-to-get-script-directory-in-posix-sh

While there is no POSIX defined standard for getting the path of a sourced script most of the shells offer some solution:
* `$BASH_SOURCE` (only for bash)
* `${.sh.file}` (only for ksh)
* `$_` (mixed results, in cases where this works it will only do so if it is run as the very first command in the sourced script)

These checks would effectively need to occur in the top level activate/deactivate scripts since the top level scripts currently call several other vital scripts. Viewing this file and you will see that no variable assignments occur there. This is because the bourne and c families do not set variables in compatible ways. This consequently means that many of the function calls are repeatedly made and while the top level activate/deactivate do perform an if-elseif-else logic it uses nothing that looks like an if-clause.

I firmly believe that `$CONDA_ROOT` is the way to go in order to quickly resolve this. The `$CONDA_PREFIX` variable is related to the activate environment. **It is set by the activate script and is likewise unset by the deactivate script.** I am proposing a new environment variable that will persist beyond activate/deactivate and always refer to the conda install location. This will also allow for the option to remove the root environment upon activate of a non-root environment (so killing two birds with one stone).

I encourage you to look over the top level activate and deactivate scripts. You should not have to look much past that file before understanding the inherent complexity in supporting these two shell families.

The debug log shows that the last action was to request an NFC-DEP listen period of 1000 ms. According to the USB dump the driver sends a TgCommRF command with receive timeout value 1000 ms and receives the chip acknowledge in the subsequent USB read. The implementation logic of USB read in transport.py polls with a max value of 500 ms (for keyboard interrupt, cause pyusb does not support the async calls). Then the second read attempt after 2 x 500 ms shows the expected chip response but at the same time the USB read logic in transport.py has seen a timeout and is in the process of looping back to another 500 ms synchronous read. Apparently the response URB after 1000 ms is now lost and the poll loop continues forever.

For me that would be okay but I think we are not allowed to change the API in minor releases so we have to wait until `0.5`. Defining `self.irradiance = self.weather` is no problem because it just sets a pointer so we will have one DataFrame with two names. In the long run one DataFrame would be enough for me.
