>It might be interesting to have df input as an optional input along with the explicit variables (which might have been what you were suggesting), but I wouldn't want to move completely to df input.

In this case the "explicit" variables would probably become kwargs, along with the new ``df`` input. Most of these kwargs would probably be set to ``None`` and then we'd need to make sure that sensible error messages inform the user when something is missing from the sum of kwargs and df inputs.

Another option is to suggest that users unpack DataFrames in the parameter call. 

Here's a quick example that uses both of the above:

```python
solpos = pd.DataFrame({'elevation':60,'zenith':30,'azimuth':180,'airmass':2.5},  index=['2015-06-21T12:00'])

    print(elevation)
    print(zenith)
    print(kwargs)
    
func_with_kwargs(**solpos)

That way I can read a yaml-file, have the tool modify the contents and then dump it back without reordering. It just seems much more intrusive to explicitly choose to sort entries alphabetically rather than just using the ordering that is native to whatever python version you are running.

@machinaut and @pat-coady  - In fact I encountered another problem, and when dealing with it I figured out an alternative way to install _mujoco-py_. Here's the story.

After I tried to install it with `sudo make`, failed and then restarted _Ubuntu 16.04_, the keyboard and mouse just wouldn't work (only in Ubuntu). 

I found that some of the dependencies in [`Dockerfile`](https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py/blob/master/Dockerfile) and [`Makefile`](https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py/blob/master/Makefile) are unnecessary and may conflict with the drivers. So I manually installed it without using **docker**, it works well on a raw Ubuntu 16.04 (I couldb't fix the drivers and had to reinstall Ubuntu):
```
git clone https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py.git
cd mujoco-py
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install libgl1-mesa-dev libgl1-mesa-glx libosmesa6-dev python3-pip python3-numpy python3-scipy
pip3 install -r requirements.txt
sudo python3 setup.py install
```

Mujoco needs an X11 server with OpenGL support in order to work. The `xvfb` package installed normally on Ubuntu 16.04 should provide it, but various things can go wrong, such as having nVidia binary drivers that don't match your hardware.

Running `glxinfo` should tell you whether OpenGL is working. If not, googling its error messages should find something.

As one of the core members of conda-forge, am interested in seeing closer collaboration between conda and wheel builders. Think both communities have a lot to gain by working together more closely. There has been some interest in this from different people in both communities. Recently have been pushing the idea of building wheels in conda-forge. ( https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-smithy/issues/608 ) That said, it is fair to characterize building wheels in conda-forge as an exploratory exercise. Thus far the results are promising. There are certainly pros and cons of the conda-forge wheel approach.

Some pros include working with the existing conda community, reusing existing infrastructure/recipes, and release processes to make packages. Configuration of CIs to upload (typically a painful process) is all automated in conda-forge and works very nicely (obviously I'm biased ;). We also build for macOS, Linux, and Windows all in one repo, which is very helpful. Anaconda.org is able to host wheels without issues. FWICT @matthew-brett has done similar things with wheels over at MacPython.

The main con would be compatibility on Linux (conda-forge uses CentOS 6 whereas manylinux1 uses CentOS 5). However these are both very old OSes and the main impact would be for legacy clusters or machines that have not upgraded (CentOS 5 EOL was last year). In practice, both builds end up being compatible on a wide range of machines. That said, I'm hopeful that a new manylinux spec would bridge the gap there. Some other changes will impact conda-forge as a whole (e.g. new compilers), but after looking into it, don't see any major impact for pyFFTW specifically.

Hope that covered all of the questions. Happy to answer more should they arise. :)

This looks like a bug to me: `buffer_info` is currently copyable, but shouldn't be.  I've submitted PR #378 to address that: it makes buffer_info movable, but not copyable (right now the compiler allows copying but not moving, but the copying results in a double-free, as you discovered).

> unique_ptr will mean we can't simply copy the buffer_info... and well, it's just info, right?

Copying a `buffer_info` won't actually copy everything: the ptr will still point to the same place, but the other fields will get copied.  I'm not sure that being able to copy makes sense given that it really *won't* be copying the data, but is rather copying a reference to it.

> I wrote some routines to extract (return) buffer_info's from a py object covering a few different cases.

What are those routines doing and returning?  In particular, in the code below, what type is `ndarray`, and what does `ndarray.acquire()` return?

> ```C++
> py::buffer_info a = ndarray.acquire(); //OK
>
> py::buffer_info a {};
> a = ndarray.acquire(); //NOT ok
> 
> py::buffer_info a {};
> a = std::move(ndarray.acquire()); //Works
> ```

I'm guessing that `acquire()` is returning a `buffer_info&`—but then the third one is stealing that reference, leaving the `buffer_info` that `ndarray` references in some indeterminate state.  If the objective here is to have `a` and `ndarray.acquire()` share data, this is almost certainly wrong—you can't depend on anything in `ndarray`'s `buffer_info` having any particular value after 3.

If my guess is correct, is there a reason to not simply take a reference to the `buffer_info` rather than trying to copy it, as in:

`py::buffer_info &a = ndarray.acquire();`

@OldPanda, here are 2 ways to do it.

The spider name is already used in the log path, it's the logfile's parent folder.
You pass the `jobid` argument to schedule.json.
This will work with the current stable release of scrapyd.
``curl http://server:port/schedule.json -d project=proj -d spider=spider -d jobid=`date +%s` ``
The log file will look like this `proj/spider/1485423127.log`
For compression, you can write a cronjob.
(FYI scrapyd also doesn't compress its own log, this is a job for logrotate)

Another way to do this is to write your own [environment](https://github.com/scrapy/scrapyd/blob/master/scrapyd/environ.py).
Currently, the environment class is not configurable

According to [this thread](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/web2py-developers/cU_ZpBAOXAU/6Hx2OPxEDuIJ), the problem noted here breaks backward compatibility and should be fixed. According to that thread, it looks like it was fixed once before, though presumably it has broken again. This issue should be re-opened.

> Or, equivalently, check to see if any messages are pending without performing an actual receive, or throwing an exception.

This is what `zmq.Poller` does. Is there a reason you aren't using polling to signal when events are available?

Yes, this is generally true with asyncio, as creating objects associated with asyncio eventloops bind references to the current eventloop when the objects are created (the `Create context and sockets` step). Changing the eventloop means that those objects are still associated with a certain eventloop, which you don't end up running. The issue seems to mainly be caused by the lack of an informative error when binding to an eventloop that won't work, which should be fixed by #988.

If you have code like:

```python
import asycio
import zmq
import zmq.asyncio

loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
ctx = zmq.asyncio.Context()
sock = ctx.socket(zmq.PUB) # <- this line will connect to asyncio.get_event_loop()

asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
```

Creating the socket would hook up to the current eventloop. After #988, this will check the type of the eventloop, and raise an error if it's not zmq's eventloop implementation.

Ah, that's confusing. pyzmq added a destroy method before zeromq. It was added as a convenience method for closing sockets and terminating the context (it does more than just term). Then zeromq decided to add an alias for zmq_term as zmq_ctx_destroy, which does the exact same thing a term. The deprecated destroy is the alternate name for term in libzmq that has been reconsidered, but the pyzmq method is not deprecated.

I guess I should give the pyzmq method a new name (perhaps `Context.close_and_term()`).

Git users can very similarly run:

Just some comments concern both the old and new implementation:

- The logic of enabling peep_holes for LSTM is ambiguous, logically you can turn on (off) the peepholes by setting W_cell of Gate to None, simple check of None should be possible to do this. This code is so bad design
```
        if self.peepholes:
            self.W_cell_to_ingate = self.add_param(
                ingate.W_cell, (num_units, ), name="W_cell_to_ingate")

            self.W_cell_to_forgetgate = self.add_param(
                forgetgate.W_cell, (num_units, ), name="W_cell_to_forgetgate")

            self.W_cell_to_outgate = self.add_param(
                outgate.W_cell, (num_units, ), name="W_cell_to_outgate")
```
What happen if we enable peephole and set W_cell to None, vice versa, setting the W_cell and have the peephole = False (it just implicitly ignore W_cell). 
=> My point is remove the argument peepholes, and check W_cell of Gate, if it is not None, we enable it (like what we did for bias in dense layer), and during the step function, simple check like _if hasattr(self, 'W_cell_to_forgetgate')_ would do a good job. User have full control to enable the peephole for only some gates they want.
```
        if self.peepholes:
            # Compute peephole connections
            ingate += cell_previous*self.W_cell_to_ingate
            forgetgate += cell_previous*self.W_cell_to_forgetgate
```

- It will be nice if the Cell have methods get_constants() which return the constants for non_sequences, one possible use case for this is same dropout mask for all time step during recurrent computation.

---

For more general design, I think we should implement the RecurrentContainer to have multiple Cell:

- A Cell can have memory (LSTM) or a non-memory cell (GRU)
- I think it is better to constraint RecurrentContainer always has **1** hidden state, and it pass this states to all Cell during computation.
- The  hidden state is passed to all cell during recurrent. Each cell will return: _[hid_new, cell_new, outputs_new]_ . _cell_new_ will be None if cell don't have memory, and the cell can return multiple outputs or no output, then outputs_new is None also. Checking this would be easy based on the init states return by CellLayer.
- The the RecurrentContainer has to handle merging multiple new hidden states, since only 1 hidden states allowed to passed.

The biggest issue is allowing inter-cells communication:
- 2 or more Cells can share their memory.
- One Cells can receives previous cell states from all other cells.
- The order of cell computation (i.e. one Cell can take the new hidden_states, cell_states, outputs_state from previous computed Cell in the step). If we handle this precisely, it is extremely complex since you have to define a mapping between cells. Simplest approach would be **parallels** or **sequential** computation option for RecurrentContainer, then mind the users about the order they add Cell to RecurrentContainer.

---

Ok, I think I figured out what the problem is here: the resets only occur if I'm hammering the topic with messages above a certain size each (it's ok for messages of 1kB, but will always crash if they're 10kB). At larger sizes, the producer worker will eventually build a `MessageSet` that the broker deems too large.

It's not possible to fix this by dialling down the `min_queued_messages` parameter (I went all the way down to `10`). The problem seems to me that the worker thread will always grab everything that's in the queue, so even if it triggers at 10 messages, when the benchmark thread is hammering the queue at the same time, the worker will eventually end up grabbing a batch of a thousand.

Can you please specify more about "expiration" and how do you process tuples?

Apache Storm (and streamparse) works like: Spout emits some new data (tuples) into topology and series of bolts will do their job of transforming the data. There is no space for "expiration".

Maybe you are talking about "failing" (NACKING) the tuple.

One way is to use for example RabbitMQ from which Spout is reading and when there is problem in topology - eg. some Bolt can't process the data, they will fail(NACK) the tuple. From here also the Spout fail the tuple and send it back to RabbitMQ (either with configured deadletter exchange or post into another exchange).

see http://streamparse.readthedocs.io/en/master/api.html#streamparse.Bolt.fail

What code did you use that reproduces this behavior? I tried the following which reproduced it:

```
> object test python
	return "comment ça va"  # this line breaks in Python 2 only
< object

+ test
- comment ça va<call>test</call>
```

The problem in the above code is actually in the `> object test python` code, which returns an ASCII string containing non-ASCII characters, and it only raises an error on Python 2. In Python 3 strings are unicode by default and the above code doesn't result in an error.

There are two workarounds if you're running RiveScript in Python 2:

1. Make your strings unicode, by putting a letter `u` in front of a quoted string, like `return u"comment ça va"`
2. Handle Unicode escaping yourself (e.g. do the `.decode("utf8")` inside your Python object macros)

Or run your bot in Python 3 if at all possible.

Here's a second update.  Now we can choose source and target max length independently to handle cases we know will be unbalanced (subword-to-char translation).  We can also use the regular max length arg to set both at once.  This update also restricts the largest bucket size to exactly max length and deduplicates buckets (can have duplicates after enforcing min size 2).

In these updates, I would like us to be able to offer 2 things to users:
* Intuitive default behavior: max length means max length on both source and target side similar to running Moses/Joshua/cdec corpus cleaning scripts
* Total control over memory usage: set source and target length independently

To bring everything back together, I would suggest a deviation arg similar to what you suggested.  A simple implementation would be something like "--ratio-deviations N" that lets users:
* Limit by source/target max length
* Kick out data with length ratio beyond N standard deviations of the average
* Automatically reset source and target max bucket size based on the retained data
This would let users choose the intuitive default behavior or the smart ratio-based optimization.  That could be a future commit.

Basically, all long options work fine, however, the script will incorrectly think short options were passed.

For `letsencrypt-auto`, all this means is sometimes you'll get the verbose output from `letsencrypt-auto` when you didn't ask for it. This happens if you run things like `letsencrypt-auto --non-interactive`, `letsencrypt-auto revoke`, `letsencrypt-auto --version`, etc. Anything that includes a `v` will trigger verbose output.

For `certbot-auto`, you have the above problem, as well as sometimes forcing yes if you didn't ask for it (if any flags you pass include an `n`). I think the worst problem though is if you don't have a venv for `certbot` and you pass a flag that includes an `h`, you'll get the `--help` output and then the script will exit.

Reloading modules in Python is not reliable enough to make it the default behaviour. Python generally assumes that modules are loaded once, and you can get all sorts of weird effects if you reload modules that aren't expecting it.

However, if you want to try reloading stuff automatically, you can use the [autoreload extension](http://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/config/extensions/autoreload.html) to do so.

A couple of questions for that proposal:

- What would merge(deep=True) do with a list?
- How would you recover the current behavior of strictly setting values of container traits? For example, `c.Class.dict_trait = {}`, which currently ensures the trait is an empty dict, overriding less-specific configuration.

We do have some of this functionality, just not in json config. *Python* config files do support modifications for container traits, which json config files do not. You can do:

```python
c.Class.dict_trait.update({'a': 5})
```

and it will add values, rather than overriding the whole trait. We could try to define a scheme for expressing such operations in JSON config, though I am wary of defining *operations* in such a format, rather than values.

Re: nb_conda_kernels, I actually really *don't* want installing a package like that to be able to change the kernel manager. I really do think installing a package and modifying configuration *should* be separate steps in the vast majority of cases. What happens when I've installed two custom kernel managers, and they have registered themselves at install time, and assume that `conda install` is everything you need to do? With conf.d-style, I'm guessing the alphabetically last manager would be used. With post-link, it would at least be the most recently installed, probably. Neither one sounds like a good experience.

This implementation adds the cache for all users of the CoordinateEncoder. Why not just let the clients determine if they want it (with a subclass that applies the decorator)? Plus if the client is using a network they would probably want to subclass the region instead and apply something similar there. Actually if there was a LearningEncoder subclass of Encoder then the RecordSensor could have an option that would enable caching for all non-learning encoders. Applying the cache when needed also removes the need for the `sizeref` hack that you added. If you go this route it would still be nice to have example code showing caching being used and an option added to the RecordSensor.

One case where the cache would not be good for the CoordinateEncoder is when it is set up to have fine-grained resolution with a large radius. In these cases, the input coordinates may very rarely match exactly and the large radius ensures that the encodings still have overlap for similar, but not exact, coordinates. In this case the caching mechanism would just slow the function down. I don't know if normal use cases would have high cache hit rates or not but perhaps @chetan51 has an intuition for it?

Yeah that's how I do it currently, in terms of storing a datetime field on the user model and just setting it to timezone.now whenever a user changes their password. Then I just validate it like this:

```class CustomJSONWebTokenAuthentication(JSONWebTokenAuthentication):
    """ Expire token on password change and force user to re-authenticate. """

    def authenticate_credentials(self, payload):
        user = super().authenticate_credentials(payload)

        orig_iat = int(payload['orig_iat'])
        password_last_changed = int(format(user.password_last_changed, 'U'))

        if orig_iat < password_last_changed:
            msg = 'Users must re-authenticate after changing password.'
            raise exceptions.AuthenticationFailed(msg)

        return user
```

I haven't run into any bugs with this, although I guess if this is part of a library that's going to be deployed in environments where server times could be out of sync or whatever then a uuid is probably more robust. (I personally like having the data about when users last changed/reset their passwords though.)

Okay, I just tried adapting the aiohttp code for this and it runs well for me.  It should preserve the `web.run_app()` functionality **exactly** while exposing the coroutines for my use case.  I will just post it here for consideration rather than making a PR.  If you like the approach, feel free to adapt and include it:

```
def run_app(app, *, host=None, port=None, path=None, sock=None,
            shutdown_timeout=60.0, ssl_context=None,
            print=print, backlog=128, access_log_format=None,
            access_log=access_logger, handle_signals=True, loop=None):
    """ Run an app locally, driving the event loop. This blocks the current thread until termination.
    """
    user_supplied_loop = loop is not None
    if loop is None:
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

    app._set_loop(loop)

    starter = app_coroutine(
        app, host=host, port=port, path=path, sock=sock,
        shutdown_timeout=shutdown_timeout, ssl_context=ssl_context,
        backlog=backlog, access_log_format=access_log_format,
        access_log=access_log)

    try:
        stopper, uris = loop.run_until_complete(starter)

        if handle_signals:
            try:
                loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGINT, raise_graceful_exit)
                loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGTERM, raise_graceful_exit)
            except NotImplementedError:  # pragma: no cover
                # add_signal_handler is not implemented on Windows
                pass

        if print:
            print("======== Running on {} ========\n"
                  "(Press CTRL+C to quit)".format(', '.join(uris)))

        try:
            loop.run_forever()
        except (GracefulExit, KeyboardInterrupt):  # pragma: no cover
            pass
        finally:
            loop.run_until_complete(stopper)
    finally:
        loop.run_until_complete(app.cleanup())

        if not user_supplied_loop:
            if hasattr(loop, 'shutdown_asyncgens'):
                loop.run_until_complete(loop.shutdown_asyncgens())
            loop.close()


async def app_coroutine(app, *, host=None, port=None, path=None, sock=None,
            shutdown_timeout=60.0, ssl_context=None,
            backlog=128, access_log_format=None,
            access_log=access_logger):
    """ Starts up an app's HTTP server and returns a co-routine to shut it down.
    """
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    await app.startup()

    make_handler_kwargs = dict()
    if access_log_format is not None:
        make_handler_kwargs['access_log_format'] = access_log_format
    handler = app.make_handler(loop=loop, access_log=access_log,
                               **make_handler_kwargs)

    server_creations, uris = _make_server_creators(
        handler,
        loop=loop, ssl_context=ssl_context,
        host=host, port=port, path=path, sock=sock,
        backlog=backlog)

    servers = await asyncio.gather(*server_creations)

    async def stopper():
        server_closures = []
        for srv in servers:
            srv.close()
            server_closures.append(srv.wait_closed())
        await asyncio.gather(*server_closures)
        await app.shutdown()
        await handler.shutdown(shutdown_timeout)

    return stopper(), uris
```

You will note that unlike `run_app`, the coroutine does not take a loop argument as it should be run on the desired loop of choice and as such it makes sense for it to get the relevant loop from context (`asyncio.get_event_loop()`).  `run_app` of course still takes the same arguments as before and accepts a different loop which is still used correctly.

Also note that I am returning the shutdown co-routine from the startup co-routine. This is because it needs to have access to some the variables the startup creates, namely `servers` and `handler`. An alternative would be to store these somewhere such as a on the `app` object, then the shutdown co-routine could be a seperate global level function that just takes the `app` as its only argument. Maybe the co-routines should even be returned by the app itself rather than be global functions...

@pfreixes I don't think the problem is the pipelined requests, in fact they are rarely used. The problem is if you are returning "Connection: keep-alive", it means you are welcome to new requests on this connection. You cannot return "Connection: keep-alive" must shutdown the connection immediately after it, it is confusing to the client side. More importantly, you cannot shutdown the connection before the first request is processed, or the client will think this is a server failure, instead of a keep-alive issue.

Detecting pending messages do work most time, but it is not a robust solution. Even if there are no pending messages in the queue, there may be a partially sent request "on the way", and the payload of the last request may not be retrieved completely.

The proposed POC is valueable, but as mentioned above, I suggest adding a "keep-alive controlling" flag, instead of detecting messages:

```python
    async def shutdown(self, timeout=15.0):
        """Worker process is about to exit, we need cleanup everything and
        stop accepting requests. It is especially important for keep-alive
        connections."""
        
        # setting this flag will make the next response to be sent with "Connection: close"
        # and prevent further processing of incoming requests
        # if we want to support more on pipelined requests, we may modify this to a counter
        self._disable_keep_alive = True

        # TODO: if the connection is upgraded to a web-socket, something may be different
        # maybe skip the waiting and directly cancel the handler? Also, `_force_close` should
        # not be set to allow the last ack on the channel.

        if timeout:
            if not self._last_request_done:
                start = self._loop.time()
                self._last_request_done_waiter = self._loop.create_future()
                with suppress(asyncio.TimeoutError):
                    await asyncio.wait_for(self._last_request_done_waiter, timeout=timeout)
                # Later the timeout will used again to wait for the last
                # incomming request. Timeout value needs to be aligned with
                # time already spend.
                timeout = max(0, timeout - (self._loop.time() - start))

        self._force_close = True
```

also, I read https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/2298#issuecomment-379268518, seems you are only testing on a single request with a designed senario - the request is completely sent and waiting for the response. But there may be more possible senarios for testing:

1. the client send a POST request with a payload a bit larger than the request buffer. The client wait for 1s, and then read the payload and response. If the `_force_close` is enabled, the request payload may be lost
2. the client sends a lot of requests on different connections at the same time (we can simply use `ab` or `wrk` to produce this), so there may be connections that are not completely processed on shutting down. When a graceful shutdown is done on gunicorn, there should not be any uncompleted/error requests - they either complete on the old worker, or complete on the new worker

For anyone else facing this issue, a simple solution is to send an extra dash at the end of the command:
`compiled_sol = compile_source(contract_source_code, import_remappings=['=/', '-'])`
The problem seems to be related to the way the solc compiler cli behaves when sending the source code via stdin AND sending the remapping arguments. The following command does not work if you try to send the source code via stdin (it returns immediately without waiting for input):
` $ solc --combined-json abi,asm,ast,bin,bin-runtime,clone-bin,devdoc,interface,opcodes,userdoc =/`
However the following does work:
` $ solc --combined-json abi,asm,ast,bin,bin-runtime,clone-bin,devdoc,interface,opcodes,userdoc =/ -`
So the simplest solution is to send an extra dash at the end of the command, and the import_remappings arguments provides an easy way to do so :)

re: really long builds: `watchman-make` is most useful when the builds are relatively short and you want to automatically run and see the results as you're hacking on something.  If the build takes a substantial amount of time, you probably do not want to have the build start automatically.   One of the challenges with adding a `--respawn-on-file-changes` option is that `watchman-make` then needs to be deal with threading to be able to manage the child process and deal with change notifications.  It's adds quite a lot of complexity and doesn't help with the most highly leveraged use case, which is to have your build + tests run quickly and automatically in another window as you work.

re: patterns question, I would phrase your invocation like this, assuming that `images` is a dir containing images:

```
.PHONY serve:
   watchman-make -p '*.tex' '*.bib' 'images/**' -t exam.pdf
```

this way you don't need to change the watchman invocation very often as you expand the scope of your document.

If the manager crashes between transaction end and before execution start, the object will be lost. To handle that, I suppose we'd have to have basically the whole `_communicate` call in a transaction, but we'd have to get rid of the queue. If there's a crash anywhere, then the started objects shouldn't roll back, which implies the transactions should be per-object. But for the duration of the manager's processing, up to the point where the process is started, things should be atomic to the outside world.

Okay, well, I don't know whether keras would add a window length of 1, I can't see any of this in the docs - what I can see, is that the keras-function `predict_on_batch` does add the batch size to the shape automatically. This would be the first 1 in your 5D array, which you have after the keras-rl function `process_state_batch` is called. Therefore, keras does complain to get a 5D array while it expects a 4D (!) array, even if you specified a 3D array shape as input shape. So, precisely the window length would be missing, as far as I see. 

In case it runs with a unity-sized window length if you add it to the input shape, but you are not sure whether it really does what it should, you could try to go to a non-unity window length, and see how it affects both program execution and training. However, you would need to either preprocess your input to fit into your model, or include for example the `TimeDistributed()` Layer of keras, and flatten everything later on, depending on your problem.

Hi, the Huawei routes can work in two mode:



1.  schema:  in this mode it can accept rpc in format like below, which have some private attribute. This is private behaviour of huawei routers 
```xml
<rpc message-id="101" xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
  <get-config>
    <source>
      <candidate/>
    </source>
    <filter>
      <ifm xmlns="http://www.huawei.com/netconf/vrp" content-version="1.0" format-version="1.0">
      </ifm>
    </filter>
  </get-config>
</rpc>
```

2.  yang: in this mode it can accept rpc in RFC format (must not have private attribute)
```xml
<rpc message-id="101" xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
  <get-config>
    <source>
      <candidate/>
    </source>
    <filter>
      <ifm xmlns="http://www.huawei.com/netconf/vrp/huawei-ifm">
      </ifm>
    </filter>
  </get-config>
</rpc>
```
The key difference in the rpc is the xmlns/format-version/content-version in the node <ifm>. And We are using <hello> to to diff this two work-mode now, if it carry any private capabilities (ex.  'http://www.huawei.com/netconf/capability/exchange/1.0') presentd in huawei.py, it will work in schema-mode;  if it carry only base 1.0 (urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.0)  OR base 1.1, it will work in yang-mode

so we need another huaweiyang.py to connect to the huawei router in yang mode

> This is caused by `validate_environment`, which doesn't make much sense.

Clearly it was a conscious choice to add that code, so just saying "it doesn't make much sense" is not the best way to start this conversation :-).

> Since `pip-accel` uses `pip` under the hood, why should I have to install another version of `pip-accel` inside every virtual environment?

When you create a virtual environment the `virtualenv` tool will automatically install a copy of `pip` inside the virtual environment. Unfortunately `pip-accel` doesn't get the same treatment, because `virtualenv` doesn't know about `pip-accel`.

In older versions of `pip-accel` this used to bite unsuspecting users which is why the check was added; mixing global installations and virtual environments can cause a wide variety of issues, some of them very obscure and non-obvious, so shielding users from this mistake seemed like the only reasonable option.

> The kicker is that if `pip-accel` is already installed globally, `pip` won't even let you install it inside a virtual environment. It's either one or the other.

This is not an absolute truth, it's a bit more subtle (IIUC):

* Old versions of `virtualenv` will (by default) create virtual environments that can also import packages that are installed system wide. This is the `--system-site-packages` command line option.
* New versions of `virtualenv` will (by default) create virtual environments that can't import packages that are installed system wide. This is the `--no-site-packages` command line option.

I assume that with the new behavior you should not be seeing this problem at all; `pip` should be able to install `pip-accel` just fine because it will realize that the `pip-accel` installed system wide is not visible inside the virtual environment.

We've been having what seems to be this issue on our servers for the past few months causing us a lot of server instability and headaches trying figure out what was going on.  For us it seems to be an issue when certain timeouts pile up, which causes raven to start consuming a lot of cpu and memory, causing more timeouts and eventually causing our server to hang.  I've created some example code to reproduce the problem:

```
import sys
import tornado.httpclient
import tornado.httpserver
import tornado.web
import tornado.gen
from raven.contrib.tornado import AsyncSentryClient, SentryMixin

#modify the time.time function so we can virtually fast-forward time.
old_time = time.time
inc_time = 0
def new_time(*args, **kwargs):
    return old_time()+inc_time
time.time = new_time

http_client = tornado.httpclient.AsyncHTTPClient()

class MainHandler(SentryMixin, tornado.web.RequestHandler):
    @tornado.gen.coroutine
    def get(self):
        global inc_time
        #simulate a long running request here by adding 30 seconds to time.time
        inc_time +=30
        #time.sleep(5)
        raise

application = tornado.web.Application([
    (r"/", MainHandler),
])
application.sentry_client = AsyncSentryClient(
    sentry_url_here,
#    enable_breadcrumbs=False,
)

def main():
    if sys.argv[1] == "0":
        application.listen(6000)
        tornado.ioloop.IOLoop.instance().start()
    elif sys.argv[1] == "1":
        count = 0
        while True:
            count+=1
            try:
                tornado.httpclient.HTTPClient().fetch("http://localhost:6000/")
            except KeyboardInterrupt:
                raise
            except:
                pass
            print count

if __name__=='__main__':
    main()
```

Run `python script.py 0` in one terminal and `python script 1` in another to reproduce the results.  When `count` reaches about 30 in the while loop, the server really starts to slow down to a crawl.  This cpu usage is being seen in the Client.encode function of base.py, as the size of the json it's trying to output reaches 100's of megabytes.  Turning off breadcrumbs seems to fix the issue.

In the script I've modified the time.time function to simulate a long running, blocking operation.  This is not entirely fair since it doesn't give any extra time to do garbage collection, and using time.sleep(30) instead seems to cause the problem to either not happen, or at least happen very slowly.  I've found that in this example, using a time.sleep(5) instead will reproduce the problem without having to modify time.time at all (in this case by `count = 20` the size of the json reaches 100k, and by `count = 60` it's about 70mb, at `count = 86` it's 1.4gb and the single tornado application is taking up 21gb of memory).

Strangely enough, when the time.sleep(5) and the modified time.time are removed, there is no issue and the json size doesn't seem to go above 35k.

The version of raven used for the above was 6.7.0, but the problem still seems to exists for older versions, even back to 5.15.0, though different versions do behave slightly differently.

Hi @akatrevorjay ,

After internal discussion:
* we really appreciate the contribution, and believe that making the cpickle version configurable is a good (great even) change.
* we want the change to make it to master, but we would like it to be as atomic and small as possible before we perform the merge.

Another way of saying this is that this is a rather low level library that many people depend on, and few people actually open up and modify. This means that the benefits of a refactor are rather low, whereas the risks of something breaking because of it would impact a large number of actors in a bad way.

Such a refactor would require us to probably bump a major version for the library, which doesn't make sense given the feature set change.

All of this being said, we all agree that your code is good and the new interface and naming make sense.

## Course of action:

We would like to open a new PR with as little changes as possible allowing for the configurable CPickle version. We can then bump that as a minor patch.

Since you've already done a lot of the work here, we're happy to make the PR changes on our side, but if you would like to do them on yours, let us know.

Cheers!

`~/.config/powerline/config.json` is a configuration file that must contain JSON dictionary. `common` is a name of the dictionary inside the top-level dictionary. `log_file` is a name of the key inside the `common` dictionary which specifies the log file.

Stderr means [stderr](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_streams#Standard_error_.28stderr.29).

And you definitely are *not* running powerline-daemon. If you were you would not get such errors in tmux.

- Account
- AccountAdministrator (som har foreignkey til settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL og til Account modellen)

Looking at the class the main functionality that is of use is:

 1. Generating the XML catalogue
 2. Generating an options file (really the same thing as 1.)
 3. Allowing a user to perform set based logic to determine which LFN they need to re-submit
 4. Get a list of PFN or LFN

I think due to 1. and 2. GaudiExec for instance will still need the LHCbDataset rather than the GangaDataset or a flat array of IGangaFile objects.
Certainly when running on a local batch system these requirements mean we can't just drop the class as it stands.


I think that replacing the `files` attribute with a 2 new attributes internally each only supporting `DiracFile` and `MassStorageFile` satisfies all of the above requirements.

Storing many DiracFile objects in a GangaList is unfortunately expensive so maybe a new version of the class which intercepts the get/set calls to the `files` attribute and silently stores the data in a more economical format (2 arrays of filenames and LFNs would be my suggestion) is more useful.

I don't think there is any requirement for the full Dirac/MassStorage-File functionality for file objects inside this class and keeping this adds some significant overheads.

I think a user might decide they want to do something with a DiracFile returned from within an LHCbDataset but I don't think it makes sense to do say `myLHCbDataset.files[1].remoteDir = someValue` as these LFN are not usually user managed.



Some very quick Pseudo-code similar to the actual PR. This should be compatible with users looking to use the `files` attribute but store much smaller objects on disk and allow for several methods to be made much simpler internally to the class.
```

class LHCbDataset:
    files: GangaArray(IGangaFiles)
    lfn_files:  GangaArray(strings)
    pfn_files: GangaArray(strings)

def __set__(value, object):
  ...
  if value == files:
    if object is DiracFile:
      self.lfn_files = object.lfn
    if object is MassStorageFile:
      self.pfn_files = object.full_path
  ...

def __get__(name):
  ...
  if name == files:
      newlist = GangaList()
      for file in self.lfn_files:
        newlist.append(DiracFile(lfn=file))
      for file in self.pfn_files:
        newlist.append(MassStorageFile(path=file))
    return newlist
  ...

```

Keep in mind that the `encoding_type` is *not* specifying the encoding of the input document. It actually affects the response -- several elements (like `Sentence` or `Token`) has the offset value, and the `encoding_type` specifies how to count those offsets. Thus it only makes sense when the users want to match the response with the input document -- and it's actually up to the user code how to handle the responses.

And by the way, the `analyzeSentimentText` seems to return the `DocumentSentiment` property only, which is per-document data and no offsets are included (thus `encoding_type` has no effects so far). It's natural to me `encoding_type` was omitted.

That isn't development names but glyph names – they can be dropped from compiled fonts since you generally just care about cmap mapping there but there are some legacy concerns, for Adobe apps mostly (something with copy/pasting text from PDFs, I think? they maintained a glyph list with PUA mappings in the days when OTL wasn't implemented properly).

In the context of GOADB, we're talking of development glyph names e.g. using horizontalbar instead of AGLFN-compliant uni2015 (that's just to carry more meaning for the designer, like Glyphs' glyph names). The replacement is then done at compile-time, with GOADB file in the case of the Adobe FDK.

Generally, I wouldn't recommend passing country/language as they have no real effect on the current parser. Language technically had a small effect in that it controlled which street-level dictionaries were searched, but that may change the model's decision thresholds, so libpostal now ignores that parameter.

Those two options were originally added as a placeholder in case predicting country/language or knowing them a priori was useful in parsing. Intuitively it seemed that must be true, but the global model performed better than one with per-country/per-language parameters. They may reappear at some point, but more for the purpose of training smaller country-specific/language-specific parser models, in which case they'd be used to select the appropriate model.

I agree with John's analysis. Mode #1 (using the `--changed-files` argument) works fine in the face of deleted files. It is mode #2 which is the issue. In more detail, this line of code:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/core/tasks/what_changed.py#L61

Does two things: generates a list of changed files (which works fine in the case of deleted files), and for each changed file, tries to map it to a target via `self._mapper.target_addresses_for_source(changedfile)`. Digging into this one, pants goes into `_find_owners()`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/base/lazy_source_mapper.py#L72

Where in a loop, it is analyzing the path of the changed file, and trying to find the nearest BUILD file. Once a candidate BUILD file has been selected, it calls `target.sources_relative_to_buildroot()`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/base/lazy_source_mapper.py#L100

This is the method that examines the actual files on disk, and maps them to a target.

The command-length splitting behaviour is covered in https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/691#issuecomment-360224845 -- there's some code hints on how to "fix" it on posix platforms, just needs some implementation!  Currently it is overly timid to account for the limitations on windows, but could be made much more permissive on platforms which provide `SC_MAX_ARG`.

Note that although there's an `activate` file written for the `rbenv`, it was [invented by pre-commit](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/834ed0f229a39c986b241374f6d338632e003b5f/pre_commit/languages/ruby.py#L68-L84) and although it used to be used, it hasn't been used since [0.8.0](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/commit/a5b56bd9e3062582f8e133999a7ffae7258d8cae) and is likely broken in subtle ways.  I've been trying to factor this out to a general purpose, cross-platform, reusable ruby virtual environment helper -- though I've been too busy so development has mostly stalled on that: https://github.com/asottile/rubyvenv/blob/master/rubyvenv.py.

A few questions here, since I think we can solve this without turning `pre-commit` into a general purpose shell :) (which I'd like to avoid).

- What command are you running specifically?
- Does the newly added `stages: [manual]` and/or `pass_filenames: false` help you out here?
- If we do this (big IF) how do we discourage (ab)use of it (it sounds too close to `run` right now (a similar confusion that beginners have with `docker`!)).  The abuse I'm describing is commands which would either break or invalidate the pre-commit metadata (think things like `pip uninstall` / `pip install` / etc.)

```python
def filesystem_is_case_insensitive():
    """From http://stackoverflow.com/a/7870076/2047682"""
    # By default mkstemp() creates a file with
    # a name that begins with 'tmp' (lowercase)
    tmphandle, tmppath = tempfile.mkstemp(dir=os.getcwd())
    case_insensitive = os.path.exists(tmppath.upper())
    os.remove(tmppath)
    return case_insensitive

def test_adding_something_with_conflict(temp_git_dir):
    if filesystem_is_case_insensitive():
        return
    with local.cwd(temp_git_dir):
        write_file('f.py', "print('hello world')")
        local['git']('add', 'f.py')
        write_file('F.py', "print('hello world')")
        local['git']('add', 'F.py')

        assert find_conflicting_filenames(['f.py', 'F.py']) == 1
```

About the tradeoffs, I would opt for a third solution: adding a a list of Python-supported file encoding as parameter to the trailing-whitespace hook.
The hook would try to open the files with each of those encoding sequentially, as long as it gets an exception.
Hence we could support popular file formats like UTF8 & Latin1, without any risk to mangle binary files, and full control over this behaviour given to the user.

Whenever you're rebasing a branch and new commit IDs get generated, you need to force-push to the remote repo, because branches that differ (new commit or ancestor IDs) can't be updated regularly:
`git push --force REMOTE-NAME BRANCH-NAME`

```
$ man git-push
...
       -f, --force
           Usually, the command refuses to update a remote ref that is not an ancestor of the local ref used to overwrite
           it. Also, when --force-with-lease option is used, the command refuses to update a remote ref whose current value
           does not match what is expected.

           This flag disables these checks, and can cause the remote repository to lose commits; use it with care.
...
```

Making TensorBoard accessible seems like a worthy pursuit - I'm sure there are ML practitioners  who are blind for instance.

The harder question seems like how. How do blind people conduct exploratory data analysis? I wonder if they rely on different hardware with say [haptic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haptic_technology) or [auditory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sound_map) affordances. How is a line chart, histogram, or image/heat map presented to a blind person?

It might be interesting for someone to sit down with a blind researcher and observe how they operate. And if it turns out that few blind researchers conduct EDA, maybe it's an opportunity and challenge for TensorBoard to bring more blind people into the fold of model interpretability.

In terms of short-term actionable items, I like William's thoughts about following web development norms like using `tabindex` and `aria-label` for now, so that blind users can at least navigate TensorBoard and perhaps enter the text dashboard.

I'd like to increase the priority on this as we encourage summary ops to be implemented as high-level TensorFlow code instead of as core TensorFlow ops. Any op that needs to process its input at all will (should) create a `name_scope` for this computation, but then we can't provide a summary op of the same name.

In the meantime, we can use the following workaround: a plugin scopes its computation and creates a summary op named `__internal_image_summary` or something (within the name scope). Then, when the plugin backend looks at any tag, it strips a suffix `/__internal_image_summary`. (Graph below.) But this is certainly not something that we want to actually require plugin authors to do, and it would be great to not have it in the graph at all, so it'd be nice to have this issue resolved before we publish these new APIs.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4317806/28226187-12e1da46-688a-11e7-90c4-2eccd27d7139.png)

Porting these models is merely a copy paste / import action (tensorpack already supports TF.slim). I wonder if it is worth spending time in porting these imagenet-based models over.
Do you really want to compete with a repo which is apparently the first choice of people -- independent of the code-readability? You cannot convince people to make a wise choice (have a look at the keras fanbase).

The work would be redundant and is first and foremost time consuming work.
And I am not sure if one needs to retrain them anyway again and again.

The [CaffeModel Zoo](
https://github.com/BVLC/caffe/wiki/Model-Zoo) is IMHO much more interesting and diverse: DeepHand, VQA, some face-recognition stuff, semantic segmentation. 

There are bigger opportunities:
My concern is that there is currently a huge gap is the tensorpack documentation of how to convert a trained model in tensorpack to production (tensorflow-serving, tf-mobile, freeze graph stuff). Since tensorpack already uses ModelDescr and has a PredictConfig being able to build the inference only graph, it would be much more straightforward to export models from tensorpack than the way described in the official TF docs (including pruning the graph). The "apply" function in the examples is almost doing it.

I can check if the tfutils/export.py still works and phrase a first documentation draft. It would be huge plus if the entire pipeline is described and supported rather than adding more image classification models.

But in the end it is: Training takes time and a proper implementation as well. I do have a mobilenet implementation and I can contribute my cityscape data reader. However, we agree that it is most of the time tedious work to just get the model correct.

This is not a wishlist of new examples (time is limited). It is a slightly different (and more broad) view on the topic.
Failing tests are just timeouts from junk. Another PR ups the timeout to 30s which will help in the future.

I spent some time today really digging into this, plowing through old release notes and talking to the PyPA people. (I owe thanks to dstufft for his oracular knowledge about packaging corner cases.) Here's what I found.

As already established, our main problem is wheels. Wheels install faster and more deterministically, but, consequently, they don't run our setup.py conditional logic; in fact, they contain no setup.py. While one can build a wheel specific to a Python version, this goes only as far as tenths-place versions; we cannot ship a wheel targeted at >=2.7.10, for instance.

Environment markers offer a way around this, but support for them is determined by the versions of 2 tools: setuptools and pip.

The setuptools version matters whenever setup.py is run. For sdists, this is at install time. For wheels, this is at build time. It must be >=17.1 (released June 2015) to support < and > operators; == is supported as early as 0.7 (mid-2013 but mysteriously missing from PyPI). As packagers of LE, we control the latter but not the former. There is a workaround to support env markers earlier: list the requirements, with markers, explicitly in setup.cfg, as [in](https://github.com/pypa/twine/blob/master/setup.cfg#L9-L15) [twine](https://github.com/pypa/twine/blob/master/setup.py#L28-L31), but that requires entirely duplicating the install_requires list and keeping it in sync, since bdist_wheel ignores the install_requires in setup.py whenever it finds a requires-dist option in setup.cfg. Because of the opportunity for error, I disregard this option below.

The second variable, the pip version, matters only for wheels. pip >=6 (December 2014) understands and obeys all environment markers laid down in wheel metadata.

All of the above matter only to people installing LE packages by hand: generally, I would hope, developers. The old le-auto solves these by updating setuptools and pip as a first step; the new, by pinning the entire dependency tree.

Combining all that, the reasonable options are...
1. Use the full-on < environment markers, and require pip >=6 and setuptools >=17.1. (Advantage: only the minimal set of requirements is installed.)
2. Use only == env markers, and require pip >= 6 and setuptools >= 0.7.
3. Use only unconditional dependencies. Conveniently, this is a workable option with our codebase. (Advantages: fewer different code paths run on different platforms, so we're likely to miss fewer bugs. Also, fewer code paths are needed in the new le-auto.)
4. Don't ship wheels at all, so our setup.py logic always has a chance to run. (Advantage: support the oldest pips and setuptools.)

Looked at the post processing in the history transcript code. I'd like to pose a question for consideration:  Do we think the transcript output would ever be too big for memory? Or to state it in another way, should we optimize for reduced memory usage or reduced i/o usage?

If we think the answer to that question is no (or reduced i/o usage), then we could perhaps be more efficient in the post processing if instead of opening the transcript file and setting it to self.stdout, we created a StringIO file (a file in memory) and set that to self.stdout. We then do the replacement on the contents of the StringIO file, and then write it all out to the specified transcript file. This would save us a read and a write of the entire transcript. Let's call this alternative 1.

If we think the answer to the question is yes (or reduced memory usage), then maybe we modify the post processing to open a temporary file and set it to self.stdout. We then open the temporary file for read, and the real transcript file for write, and read the lines one at a time, do the replacement, and write them to the real transcript file. Thereby never having to have the entire transcript in memory. Let's call this alternative 2.

Reality is likely that nobody would notice whether we leave it as is, or do one of these other two options. One benefit I like of the current implementation is that there is no reliance on a temporary file, which I think is a drawback of alternative 2. Alternative 1 depends on the `six` library for compatibility between python 2 and 3, because the StringIO library is different.

Would use of this feature (at least for use case 1) imply that the return value is cached for some (configurable) period of time? It would seem a bit arbitrary to me if a call takes exactly 1 second, and if a second call comes 0.99 seconds later, it would return the value for the first call, whereas if it came 1.01 seconds later, it would trigger a new call. If 1.01 seconds is long enough to want a new call, it seems like you'd want the one 0.99 seconds to trigger a new call as well. Basically, I'm suggesting that the "staleness" be provided by the user rather than using however long the first call takes, which could vary.

Right, there are two issues here: (1) what we should be doing with `IPV6_V6ONLY` by default (which overlaps with #72), and (2) fixing the exception.

Am I reading your table correctly as saying that the problem happens when you set up a listen socket with `IPV6_V6ONLY` set to False, then accept a v4 connection, and then trio tries to set `IPV6_V6ONLY` on the new labeled-as-ipv6-but-secretly-actually-a-v4-socket? That makes a lot of sense :-). So at the very least we should tolerate an error here.

As to the larger question of defaults, there are a few competing considerations:

The problem with accepting the system default is that it leads to non-portable programs (in particular in this case: test on linux, everything works fine because in fact everyone leaves `bindv6only` set to 0, and then you're broken on windows because they make `IPV6_V6ONLY` default to enabled. And in practice, setting V6ONLY=False doesn't actually make programs magically handle ipv4 and ipv6 equally, because e.g. people expect that their web server's connection logs will contain ipv4 addresses rather than ipv6-mapped-ipv4 addresses. So I figured better to have consistency and then let people pick if they want something different... libuv does something similar, though IIRC they have the opposite default from trio's current setting. I don't have a strong opinion on which default is better :-)

But then there's another issue: initially I hoped that `trio.socket` would be the standard user-level API for doing networking in trio, but in the course of implementing SSL support have come around to the conclusion that we need a higher-level layer that provides a dedicated "stream" abstraction. There's a huge work-in-progress branch in #107 with the initial part of this. As part of this, I've defined a `SocketStream` class that adapts a socket into the `Stream` interface; and probably I will also add high-level helpers for connecting to a host+port and for listening on a port, that are defined at this level. So... if `trio.socket` is becoming the "low-level raw" networking API, maybe it does make more sense to remove the default-setting code from here, and instead handle it in the higher-level layer. (So in particular, the "listen on this port" interface would automatically create both ipv4 and and ipv6 sockets, and transparently listen on both of them.)

Thanks @surehb @eric-jj, this is a great idea and so far we have been neglecting it! I have a few high-level comments.

1. I don't think driver heartbeats are necessary, since we can detect whenever a driver disconnects from the node manager (and we can also store the mapping from driver ID to node manager ID).
2. Any new information that is added to the GCS should be done through the "tables" interface (unless there is a good reason not to). This provides a unified way to store/retrieve information from Redis and will be shared between Python/Java. See https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/src/ray/gcs/tables.h. Stuff that is currently in pure Python can stay that way for now (but should eventually be migrated).
3. About the implementation. The code for "adding" stuff to the GCS and the code for "removing" stuff from the GCS are coupled in the sense that they each have to have knowledge of the underlying schema. Therefore, it makes sense for both of these functionalities to be implemented in the same place. The "table" API is the natural way to do this, but for the stuff that is in pure Python, adding to and removing from a specific table should possibly be part of the same class.
4. Some of the schemas will probably need to be redesigned in the future.
5. Lastly, let's implement all of this functionality in multiple PRs. In particular, I think the following can be done separately
    - Detecting driver deaths. We started working on this in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/2289, but it looks like it didn't get finished.
    - Doing cleanups for the exports (also related to #2289).
    - Doing cleanups for the task table and object table (also related to #2289).
    - Other cleanups.

Just pushed a commit which should fix the jenkins test. Basically, the PR changed the behavior so that now actor classes are exported to Redis as soon as the actor class is defined (before this happened as soon as the first actor object was instantiated). The problem is that we can only export to Redis as soon as `ray.init()` has been called, so if the actor class is defined before `ray.init()` is called, then we need to cache it and export it once `ray.init()` is called.

As an aside, my entire business need was to select only a fraction of the columns, and so I've now accomplished that using the code below:

```
fname = "mycsv.csv"

session.submit(job, results="Text")
data = session.sasdata( "%s_2" % table_name, "MYLIB", results='Text')
data.to_df_CSV(tempfile=fname, tempkeep=True)
```

Our approach to fix this issue before is to patch some modules in runtime, in ``djmoney.apps.MoneyConfig.ready``. 

But if problematic function (``display_for_field``) will be imported before this patch, then it will not be patched in this module, where was imported. Thus being said in the worst case we can't guarantee, that our patching will cover all imports - for example if you'll import ``display_for_field`` in your code, that is loaded before ``djmoney``, then you'll use not patched version we can't detect it automatically.

Consequently, ``django.contrib.admin.templatetags.admin_list`` could also be affected, because it imports ``display_for_field``. Anyway, I'll push the fix in a few minutes.

Feedback from Prof. Fienup:

When comparing complex-valued images (which we often do for optical fields), the Python version does not always work, whereas the old Matlab version does work, as described in the emails below.

This might be due to what Alex mentions in his Feb. 18 email:

When the upsampling factor is greater than 1, Manuels routine finds an initial shift estimate using an upsampling factor of 2. The Python code uses an upsampling factor of 1 for all initial guesses. I tried modifying the Python code to find an estimate using an upsampling factor of 2, and that seemed to solve the problem for a range of piston values (-pi/2, pi/2).

So initially doing an FFT with 2x embedding in each dimension seems to be the safer thing to do, although it is more computationally expensive.

Best regards,

For the `matrix` -> `transform` case, it appears to happen due to a precision reduction.  AFAICT, the original SVG has:

```xml
<g
     id="g3787"
     transform="matrix(1.0000227,0,0,1,-4.7728357e-4,0)>"
```

This is reduced to:

```xml
<g id="g3787" transform="matrix(1 0 0 1 -.00047728 0)">
```
(Note the first parameter in matrix is reduced to a plan 1).  Once the matrix has pure 0 and 1 integer parameters, then it can apply the rewrite from `optimizeTransform` (which happens in the second run).

If the first precision rewrite is acceptable, then we should be able to just apply the same level of "fuzziness" in `optimizeTransform` before testing for the known patterns.

The result object you get back from `execute` has an errors attribute that contains an array of errors that happened during the execute phase. You can use that to report errors to sentry. E.g.:

```python
for error in result.errors:
	# error is an instance of `GraphQLLocatedError` where the original error
	# is on the `original_error` attribute
	try:
    	raise error.original_error
    except Exception:
    	sentry_client.captureException()
```

Or you can configure your logging setup to propagate errors from `graphql.execution.executor` to sentry. They are logged out here: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/b3a6bd0f89651af10a937e5270e46c696672a2e5/graphql/execution/executor.py#L314

`Paragraph._element` is a "private" variable, which is why it doesn't appear in the API documentation. "Private" here reflects its role as an implementation detail, and these can be changed at will as required to add new features or refactor or whatever. So theoretically this is subject to change without notice, but I'd say these `._element` names are very unlikely to change going forward. Any time you use "internals", or more precisely "implementation specifics", you risk breakage when you upgrade, but I'd put use of these at the low end of that risk spectrum.

The `.getparent()`, `.remove()`, and `append()` methods are on `lxml.etree._Element`, which is the library `python-docx` uses for XML manipulation. You can find the lxml API documentation online here http://lxml.de/, look for 'API Documentation' on the left hand navigator to access the latest version.

class CT_P(BaseOxmlElement):
    """
    ``<w:p>`` element, containing the properties and text for a paragraph.
    """
    pPr = ZeroOrOne('w:pPr')
    r = ZeroOrMore('w:r')

the `text\paragraph.py` file:
```python
from __future__ import (
    absolute_import, division, print_function, unicode_literals
)

from ..enum.style import WD_STYLE_TYPE
from .parfmt import ParagraphFormat
from .run import Run
from ..shared import Parented

from docx.shared import ElementProxy
from docx.oxml.bookmark import CT_BookmarkStart, CT_BookmarkEnd 

class Paragraph(Parented):
    """
    Proxy object wrapping ``<w:p>`` element.
    """
    def __init__(self, p, parent):
        super(Paragraph, self).__init__(parent)
        self._p = self._element = p
    
    def add_bookmark_start(self, id, name):
        bmrk = self._p._add_bookmarkStart()
        bmrk.name = name
        bmrk.bmrk_id = str(id)
        return self._p.append(bmrk) 
    
    def add_bookmark_end(self, id):
        bmrk = self._p._add_bookmarkEnd()
        bmrk.bmrk_id =str(id)
        return self._p.append(bmrk)
```

class CT_BookmarkEnd(BaseOxmlElement):

For anyone needing a workaround you can use this function.

```python
def add_hyperlink(paragraph, url, text):
    """
    A function that places a hyperlink within a paragraph object.

    :param paragraph: The paragraph we are adding the hyperlink to.
    :param url: A string containing the required url
    :param text: The text displayed for the url
    :return: A Run object containing the hyperlink
    """

    # This gets access to the document.xml.rels file and gets a new relation id value
    part = paragraph._parent._parent.part
    r_id = part.relate_to(url, RT.HYPERLINK, is_external=True)

    # Create the w:hyperlink tag and add needed values
    hyperlink = OxmlElement('w:hyperlink')
    hyperlink.set(qn('r:id'), r_id, )
    hyperlink.set(qn('w:history'), '1')

    # Create a w:r element
    new_run = OxmlElement('w:r')

    # Create a new w:rPr element
    rPr = OxmlElement('w:rPr')

    # Create a w:rStyle element, note this currently does not add the hyperlink style as its not in
    # the default template, I have left it here in case someone designs their own
    rStyle = OxmlElement('w:rStyle')
    rStyle.set(qn('w:val'), 'Hyperlink')

    # Join all the xml elements together add add the required text to the w:r element
    rPr.append(rStyle)
    new_run.append(rPr)
    new_run.text = text
    hyperlink.append(new_run)

    # Create a new Run object and add the hyperlink into it
    r = paragraph.add_run()
    r._r.append(hyperlink)

    # A workaround for the lack of a hyperlink style (doesn't go purple after using the link)
    r.font.color.theme_color = MSO_THEME_COLOR_INDEX.HYPERLINK
    r.font.underline = True

    return r
```

@nkbai Thanks for the issue report.

You're correct, the [code](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ca2564bb7043c345ab44325dce5b3a3b0906f51f/raiden/token_swap.py#L608) is not taking the taker settle timeout.

```python
lock_expiration = maker_paying_transfer.lock.expiration - raiden.config['reveal_timeout']
```

The correct fix however, is to use the minimum of channel used by the *taker* for the second mediated transfer:

```python
max_lock_expiration = taker_paying_channel.settle_timeout + raiden.get_block_number()
expiration_blocks = min(max_lock_expiration, maker_paying_transfer.lock.expiration)
lock_expiration = expiration_blocks - raiden.config['reveal_timeout']
```

There should be no additional problems with longer hops.

Here's a guess at what is going on:

1. When clicking to do a selection, https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/1bc4a6765565fe39290c4007a3c77a4639bf3c75/packages/controls/src/widget_selection.ts#L789 is run, which sets the selected index. This causes https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/1bc4a6765565fe39290c4007a3c77a4639bf3c75/packages/controls/src/widget_selection.ts#L770 to run, which resets the selected index programmatically. Somehow in older browsers like noted above, that causes a scroll in the select box. One possible solution is to pass in an argument to the model index set giving the view doing the selection, and then in the update method checking to see if that is the current view. That's a pattern we use several other places, like https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/1bc4a6765565fe39290c4007a3c77a4639bf3c75/packages/controls/src/widget_selection.ts#L542

> Awesome. Looks like flask-apispec uses the swagger module, so that will need to be updated. Are you just moving those functions to apispec.ext.marshmallow?

No. I'm cramming them into a `Swagger` class (or `OpenAPIConverter`, or any better name). This class has attributes: 

- list of schema references (or should it be in `MarshmallowPlugin`?)
- openapi version

This indeed breaks code that uses functions from swagger.py file, such as `flask-apispec` or our `flask-rest-api` but hopefully, it should be an easy fix: just instantiate the class and use it.

Current design is wrong because `spec` is transmitted all along the chain, unless you don't need the schema references so you don't care about the `spec`. Except now, we also need the openapi version potentially everywhere. We could pass the version, and the refs, and next, what? Making all those class methods makes it practical for that.

Using those functions directly is wrong because when not passing a spec object, version defaults to OpenAPI 2. And to specify version 3, you need to create a spec with openapi version set to 3, which is ridiculous. At least that's my understanding. I'm not claiming I'm 100% sure about everything, here. Hopefully, it'll be clearer when I'm done.

Overall, it will be breaking, not backward compatible, but easy to fix from client side. And since it is a breaking change, I'd rather be sure it is correct before releasing.

> Also, do we want to allow passing string paths to the new plugin classes?

I'd rather not.

- Little benefit: you don't need to import + instantiate a class. That's a small gain. I don't think it's worth the added complexity (which is small as well, I admit).
- Currently, I keep backward compatibility by checking whether input is a string (old interface) or an object (new interface). Doing what you suggest makes it harder to discriminate old/new.
- Two ways to achieve the same thing. Shrug. When I do that, I can almost feel the presence of a mini @deckar01 floating above my shoulder telling me not to do it, and I know he's right.
- Plugin classes may receive parameters, such as the `schema_name_resolver` callable passed to `MarshmallowPlugin`, so this won't work anymore (unless we add another layer of complexity).

Hopefully Rob cleared things up for you. In the code that you pasted, I think I found a spot where your thinking is different from what Cass is doing:

    match_one = matchobject
    dyrus=match_one.participant[0]     ### the first participant of this match is dyrus

This is all good and I think you understand what is going on.

    champstat=dyrus.summoner.ranked_stats(season=Season.season_5)[dyrus.champion]

On the above line, `dyrus` is a `Participant` in `match_one`. However, `dyrus.summoner` is THE `Summoner` Dyrus, not the participant in `match_one`. The summoner Dyrus participated in `match_one`, but he does not "belong" to it. `Participants` in a `Match` have stats like `gold`, but when you talk about Dyrus as a Summoner, he doesn't have "gold" -- he has a name, an ID, a number of Riot Points (which we can't see), a match history (that we can pull from the Riot API), etc. The `Summoner` Dyrus is different than the `Participant` Dyrus in `match_one`.

So when you do `dyrus.summoner.ranked_stats(...)`, you are *not* getting Dyrus's season 5 ranked statistics of the champion he played *for match_one*. You are getting Dyrus's season 5 ranked statistics for *every match he has played in season 5*.
We should do what you said and return list in response instead of sets.

> To play devil's advocate to your devil's advocate :)

That's usually called angel's advocate.

> I am a bit loathe to rearrange some pre-existing and smoothly working functionality just to get a nice unit test. ChromaticObject already works well and has quite a few unit tests!

I meant that as a unit test of the new interpolating functionality.  Not the old ChromaticObject behavior.  Also, I think most of the rearrangement would be in the new stuff.  The code path for things that don't ever call `setupInterpolation` could be made to be essentially unchanged.

> I feel like having a drawImage routine that just says "if BLAH: go to THIS function, else: go to THAT function" is kind of ugly.

Python lets you do something a bit prettier than that, since you can define methods on the fly.  You could have the `setupInterpolation` function swap in the interpolating version of drawImage so any calls to `obj.drawImage(...)` would use that code instead.  

But regardless, I'm more interested in the front end design than the prettiness of the implementation.  We should decide which API makes more sense first, and then implement that, rather than pick an API that has an implementation we happen to prefer.

> drawImage has different optional arguments for ChromaticObject and InterpolatedChromaticObject right now

Only 2 arguments: `force_eval` and `integrator`.  I think `force_eval` would be unnecessary in my proposed paradigm.  And we can just say that `integrator` will be ignored if the user has previously called `setupInterpolation`.

> They will be in different directories presumably.

> I guess the new catalog will include everything in the 23.5 catalog plus more, right?

I omitted an important detail.  The new catalog is a *fair subset* of all galaxies brighter than 25.2.  In practice, when we were doing the parametric fits for this fainter sample, it was pretty expensive to do everything in COSMOS, so we took a subset.  For that reason, if you were to take the new catalog and use only the <23.5 galaxies, you'd get something quite a bit smaller than the current catalog.  (New catalog has ~88k galaxies with I<25.2, while the current catalog has 56k with I<23.5.)

Hi all,

I agree on @dbarrosop's points, it's a hack and it shouldn't be done this way, but I thought it would be better to work it around in napalm, instead on the upstream python-eznc 'API', which could break an unknown number of downstream libraries. I guess @dbarrosop is right there, that's for py-eznc to decide.

So, before I go and check how to fix it upstream, I dug down a bit on what actually stops me from using napalm-ansible with the junos driver and the "no-password, no-key" strategy that would trigger setting allow_agent in py-eznc.

Culprit seems to be that in napalm-ansible, [password is checked for None](https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm-ansible/blob/develop/napalm_ansible/napalm_get_facts.py#L208-L209) (e.g. for get_facts), but the password is a not required option according to function's doc.

As soon as I removed password from this dict, I was able to login (hence allow_agent setting in pyeznc was triggered) and get_facts worked with the following:
```
  - name: lala
    napalm_get_facts:
      hostname="switchname"
      username=costasd
      dev_os=junos
      optional_args='ssh_config_file=ssh_config'
      filter='facts'
```

So, it seems that the real culprit is napalm-ansible functions ensuring password is not None, while their doc says that password is `required: false`

Would that be an acceptable fix on napalm-ansible? (I'll open a new PR)

Feel free to reject this one, thanks for your time!

To use GCE and Cloud storage

1. Go to your Project credentials
2. If you don't already have a service account and json/p12 key, you can create a new one then generate .p12  or .json file.
3. Copy it to your compute engine
4. You can use classmethod from_service_account_p12 or classmethod from_service_account_json respectively and copy it to your compute engine directory.For the 'email' parameter in the classmethod from_service_account_p12 you will use the service account email that you've generated the key from. You will include the link to the file in either of the classmethod parameters.
5. You then have to assign read-only or ownership to whatever file/directory you're trying to access  in the cloud storage browser or through command line
6. You will then have to establish the bucket (if you're only working within one bucket) with gcloud.storage.bucket.Bucket(name='BucketName')
7.  To initialize a blob object you will have to use 
 blob = gcloud.storage.blob.Blob(blobname, bucket=bucket, chunk_size=None)

Two comments:
1. taskqueue.TombstonedTaskError and taskqueue.TaskAlreadyExistsError don't protect against all duplication of messages. IE a message could be received, processed, and then fail to be acknowledged. So the library code still needs to be idempotent. (by updating datastore)

2. It would probably be much better to manage retries externally to the task queue. IE: set the taskqueue retry limit to infinite, and manage the sate in datastore. This would avoid the problem of having an inconsistent state between them resulting in silently stopping.
